label: Chamber debate (House of Lords)
name: Chamber debate
description: Debates in the House of Lords Chamber take place for affirmative instruments on a motion that "the instrument be approved". Debates in the Chamber can also take place for negative instruments, there are three possible motions for such instruments - that "an Humble Address" be presented to Her Majesty praying that the instrument be annulled; motions inviting the House to call on the government to take action or record a particular point of view, without annulling the instrument itself; or a neutral "take note" motion.
actualisationnote: Actualise this step when it is scheduled on the Parliamentary Calendar or Future Business so it appears in 'Scheduled upcoming stages' on the workpackage page. The link will need to be updated once the debate has taken place. If it's rescheduled, change the date.
datenote: Date of debate

label: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion falls at dissolution (Senedd Cymru)
name: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion falls at dissolution

label: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument being law rejected (House of Commons)
name: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument being law rejected
description: A statutory instrument laid under the made negative procedure becomes law on the day the Minister signs it and automatically remains law unless a motion – or ‘prayer’ – to reject it is agreed by either House within 40 sitting days of the instrument being laid.  In the Commons, such motions are usually tabled as an Early Day Motion.
actualisationnote: Will usually follow a Commons debate, could be grouped together with the actual approval motion.
datenote: Date of decision

label: Delegated Legislation Committee (DLC) debate (House of Commons)
name: Delegated Legislation Committee (DLC) debate
description: Debates in a Delegated Legislation Committee take place on a motion that the Committee has considered the instrument or treaty referred to it.
actualisationnote: Actualise this step when it is scheduled on the Parliamentary Calendar or Future Business so it appears in 'Scheduled upcoming stages' on the workpackage page. The link will need to be updated once the debate has taken place. If it's rescheduled, change the date.
linknote: Start of debate in Commons Hansard
datenote: Date of debate

label: Instrument approved (House of Lords)
name: Instrument approved
description: An affirmative instrument approved by the House of Lords.
actualisationnote: To be added when the House of Lords approves an instrument. If the Commons have already approved then please actualise the bicameral step too. Do not add the bicameral step if the Commons have not approved.
datenote: Date of decision

label: Instrument approved (House of Commons)
name: Instrument approved
description: An affirmative instrument approved by the House of Commons.
actualisationnote: To be added when the House of Commons approves an instrument. If the instrument is laid in HoC only then it must be actualised alongside the 'Instrument approved by the House of Commons' step. If the instrument is also laid in the Lords and Lords have already approved then please actualise the bicameral step too. Do not add the bicameral step if the Lords have  not approved.
datenote: Date of decision

label: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument withdrawn (House of Lords)
name: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument withdrawn
description: A non-fatal motion does not stop a statutory instrument being law but may be used by either House to indicate concern. The most common non-fatal motion in the Lords is a motion to regret. The motion usually gives specific reasons for the regret. Even if agreed, the motion cannot stop or amend the statutory instrument, but gives members an opportunity to put on record their dissent.
actualisationnote: Motions can be withdrawn at any stage before debate or they will be withdrawn at the end of debate either singularly or grouped.
linknote: No link if withdrawn before a debate takes place (motion will simply disappear) otherwise if it is withdrawn following a debate then the LBP minute link
datenote: Date withdrawn

label: Question on non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument put (House of Commons)
name: Question on non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument put
description: A non-fatal motion (prayer) does not stop a statutory instrument being law but may be used by either House to indicate concern. In the Commons, a prayer motion tabled outside the 40 sitting days period is non-fatal as it can only object to the instrument, rather than stop it.
actualisationnote: Will usually follow a debate or as a separate motion following a DLC debate. The decision could also be deferred.
datenote: Date question put

label: Instrument rejected (House of Lords)
name: Instrument rejected
description: Instrument rejected by the House of Lords.  If either House resolves that an affirmative instrument should not be approved, the instrument, depending on whether it is a draft or already made, cannot become law or stops being law.
actualisationnote: If actualised this will bring the SI procedure to an end.
datenote: Date of decision

label: Not drawn to the special attention of the Houses by the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments (JCSI) (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Not drawn to the special attention of the Houses by the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments (JCSI)
description: Instrument considered by the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments and no concerns found.
actualisationnote: Will appear under the report heading "Instruments not reported". We will first see notice of the report in the Vote/LBP on a Thursday but the report will be published on a Friday so we actualise retrospectively. Name changed from 'no concerns raised' in March 2020. Name changed from 'instrument not reported' in October 2023.
datenote: Date the JCSI met (usually a Wednesday).

label: Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee (SLSC) recommended the instrument should follow the affirmative procedure (House of Lords)
name: Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee (SLSC) recommended the instrument should follow the affirmative procedure
description: The Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee, under the remit of Schedule 7 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, recommended that the appropriate procedure for the instrument is for a draft of it be laid before, and approved by a resolution, of each House before it is made.
actualisationnote: We will first see notice of the report in the Vote/LBP on a Wednesday but the report will be published on a Thursday so we actualise retrospectively.
datenote: Date the SLSC met (usually a Tuesday or Wednesday depending on which Sub-Comm).

label: Drawn to the special attention of the House by the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee (SLSC) (House of Lords)
name: Drawn to the special attention of the House by the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee (SLSC)
description: Instrument considered by the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee which raised concerns in line with the committee's terms of reference.
actualisationnote: Will appear under the report heading "Instruments drawn to the special attention of the House". We will first see notice of the report in the Vote/LBP on a Wednesday but the report will be published on a Thursday so we actualise retrospectively. If the SLSC includes a previously considered instrument in another report with a different outcome please check with Jane White. While this is possible it is not considered usual practice and must be checked before we actualise any further steps.
linknote: SLSC report - anchor link to specific instrument
datenote: Date the SLSC met (usually a Tuesday or Wednesday depending on which Sub-Comm).

label: Instrument can be made (signed into law)
name: Instrument can be made (signed into law)
description: Draft negative instruments can be made once they have sat before the House(s) for 40 days and a fatal prayer has not been approved. Draft affirmative instruments can be made once the House(s) have approved the instrument.
actualisationnote: Applies to draft instruments that have completed their procedure but have not been signed by Govt minister.
datenote: Date clock ends or date procedure concluded in the House.

label: Instrument remains law
name: Instrument remains law
description: Made negative instruments remain law once they reach the end of the 40 day objection period and neither House has objected. Made affirmative instruments remain law once they have been approved by the House(s).
actualisationnote: Previously "Instrument remains in effect as law", which was inaccurate for made negatives which come into force after the objection period ends. It is only accurate for made negatives which come into force during the objection period.
datenote: Date clock ends

label: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion not selected by Presiding Officer (Senedd Cymru)
name: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion not selected by Presiding Officer

label: Drawn to the special attention of the Houses by the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments (JCSI) (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Drawn to the special attention of the Houses by the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments (JCSI)
description: Instrument considered by the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments and has raised concerns in line with the committee's terms of reference.
actualisationnote: Will appear under the heading 'Instruments reported' in the actual report. We will first see notice of the report in the Vote/LBP on a Thursday but the report will be published on a Friday so we actualise retrospectively. Name changed from 'concerns raised' in March 2020. Name changed from 'instrument reported' in October 2023.
linknote: JCSI report - anchor link to the specific instrument
datenote: Date the JCSI met (usually a Wednesday).

label: Considered by the European Statutory Instruments Committee (ESIC) (House of Commons)
name: Considered by the European Statutory Instruments Committee (ESIC)
description: Consideration completed by the European Statutory Instruments Committee within their terms of reference. The committee was active from July 2018 until the 29 May 2024 when the 58th Parliament was dissolved.
actualisationnote: We will first see notice of the report in the Vote/LBP on a Wednesday but the report will be published on a Thursday so we actualise retrospectively.  The committee's functions have been replaced by departmental committees.
linknote: Depends on the outcome of the report for each instrument.
datenote: Date ESIC met (usually a Tuesday)

label: Not certified under the English votes for English laws (EVEL) process (House of Commons)
name: Not certified under the English votes for English laws (EVEL) process
description: The English Votes for English Laws (EVEL) process is designed to ensure that legislation that affects only England, or England and Wales, is approved by a majority of MPs representing English constituencies, or English and Welsh constituencies. It also applies to legislation introducing a tax measure that affects only England, Wales and Northern Ireland, which must be approved by a majority of MPs representing constituencies in those areas. Instruments not certified apply to the whole of the UK.
actualisationnote: We receive this information via email from the Speaker's Office. Usually once a week but could be adhoc or more than once.
datenote: Date of the Speaker's consideration - will be on the letter we receive from the Speaker's Office

label: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument rejected (House of Commons)
name: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument rejected
description: A non-fatal motion (prayer) does not stop a statutory instrument being law but may be used by either House to indicate concern. In the Commons, a prayer motion tabled outside the 40 sitting days period is non-fatal as it can only object to the instrument, rather than stop it.
actualisationnote: Motion text will refer to "revoke" or "withdraw". Will usually follow a debate in the Chamber or as a formal proceeding following a DLC debate.
datenote: Date of the decision

label: Instrument rejected (House of Commons)
name: Instrument rejected
description: Affirmative instrument rejected by the House of Commons. If either House resolves that an affirmative instrument should not be approved, the instrument, depending on whether it is a draft or already made, cannot become law or stops being law.
actualisationnote: If actualised this will bring the SI procedure to an end.
datenote: Date of decision

label: Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee (SLSC) agreed the instrument should follow the negative procedure (House of Lords)
name: Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee (SLSC) agreed the instrument should follow the negative procedure
description: The Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee, under the remit of Schedule 7 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, agreed that the appropriate procedure for the instrument is for it to be subject to annulment.
actualisationnote: We will first see notice of the report in the Vote/LBP on a Wednesday but the report will be published on a Thursday so we actualise retrospectively.
datenote: Date the SLSC met (usually a Tuesday or Wednesday depending on which Sub-Comm).

label: Non-ten minute rule Public Bill created
name: Non-ten minute rule Public Bill created
description: Bills introduced by leave of the House following a speech of not more than ten minutes from the sponsor of the proposal.

label: Proposed negative statutory instrument created
name: Proposed negative statutory instrument created
description: This shows the text of the instrument. This step is not part of parliamentary procedure, it is an indicative starting step for the purposes of the Statutory Instrument Service website.
actualisationnote: Individual step that begins the PNSI procedure
datenote: Date laid

label: Decision on motion to approve the instrument deferred (House of Commons)
name: Decision on motion to approve the instrument deferred
description: After the moment of interruption (the time at which the main business must usually finish), any division on a statutory instrument motion is deferred until the following Wednesday.
actualisationnote: Will usually appear in the Vote along the lines of "The Deputy Speaker’s opinion as to the decision of the Question was challenged. "
datenote: Date the question was put

label: Scrutiny reserve for the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments (JCSI) dispensed with (House of Lords)
name: Scrutiny reserve for the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments (JCSI) dispensed with
description: Under House of Lords Standing Order 72, no motion for a resolution of the House to approve an affirmative instrument shall be made unless the instrument has been considered by the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments. Standing Order 72 can be dispensed with to allow motions to approve affirmative instruments. This usually only applies to emergency legislation.
actualisationnote: "Refers to SO No 73 in the Lords. The SO reserves a period of scrutiny to the JCSI, during which approval motion cannot be tabled, until such time as the JCSI has reported on the instrument. Suspension of this SO removes the bar on tabling an approval motion before reporting. In other words, the approval motion can be be moved despite the JCSI not having yet reported. "
datenote: Date on which the SO is suspended

label: Question put on fatal amendment to legislative consent motion (Senedd Cymru)
name: Question put on fatal amendment to legislative consent motion

label: Instrument withdrawn from the House of Lords (House of Lords)
name: Instrument withdrawn from the House of Lords
description: Government has withdrawn the draft instrument from the House of Lords and therefore procedure has concluded.
actualisationnote: Instrument must be withdrawn from both Houses if bicameral and this will also result in the procedure concluding. Note that instruments can be laid into the House of Lords accidentally when it should only be a Commons only instrument, DO NOT add the withdrawn step in these cases, please delete all Lords steps from the workpackage as if they never happened.
datenote: Date withdrawn

label: Instrument withdrawn from the House of Commons (House of Commons)
name: Instrument withdrawn from the House of Commons
description: Government has withdrawn the draft instrument from the House of Commons and therefore procedure has concluded.
actualisationnote: Instrument must be withdrawn from both Houses if bicameral and this will also result in the procedure concluding.
datenote: Date withdrawn

label: Proposed negative statutory instrument withdrawn from the House of Lords (House of Lords)
name: Proposed negative statutory instrument withdrawn from the House of Lords
description: Government has withdrawn the proposed negative statutory instrument from the House of Lords and therefore procedure has concluded.
actualisationnote: A PNSI being withdrawn will result in the procedure concluding in both Houses.
linknote: Individual item in the Lords Papers
datenote: Date withdrawn

label: Proposed negative statutory instrument withdrawn from the House of Commons (House of Commons)
name: Proposed negative statutory instrument withdrawn from the House of Commons
description: Government has withdrawn the proposed negative statutory instrument from the House of Commons and therefore procedure has concluded.
actualisationnote: A PNSI being withdrawn will result in the procedure concluding in both Houses.
datenote: Date withdrawn

label: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion agreed to (Senedd Cymru)
name: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion agreed to

label: Procedure concluded in the House of Commons (House of Commons)
name: Procedure concluded in the House of Commons
description: Parliamentary procedure has concluded for this House of Commons only instrument. It is unlikely that any further scrutiny will take place, however there is a possibility that non-substantive business, such as a debate or committee consideration, could take place.
actualisationnote: Applies to House of Commons only SIs. Do not use if the instrument has been laid in the Lords, there is a specific bicameral step.
datenote: Depends on the SI procedure - Made negatives should be actualised on the date objection period ends. For affirmatives when the House of Commons approves/rejects the SI

label: Procedure concluded in the House of Commons and the House of Lords (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Procedure concluded in the House of Commons and the House of Lords
description: Parliamentary procedure has concluded for this bicameral instrument. It is unlikely that any further scrutiny will take place, however there is a possibility that non-substantive business, such as a debate or committee consideration, could take place.
actualisationnote: Applies to all SIs/PNSIs laid in both Houses
datenote: Dependent on the SI procedure - negatives depends on the end of the objection period. For affirmatives it is dependent on the approval/rejection of the last House to put the question.

label: Bill in Commons as first House
name: Bill in Commons as first House
description: Bill introduced to Parliament by a Member of the House of Commons.

label: Ministerial statement under Section 22 of the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010: Section 20 does not apply (House of Lords)
name: Ministerial statement under Section 22 of the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010: Section 20 does not apply
description: Allows a Minister to certify that a treaty can be ratified although it has not met the requirements of section 20 of CRAG.  It applies in ‘exceptional circumstances’. These are not defined. The only restriction on a Minister using the power under section 22 is if either House has already resolved that the treaty should not be ratified.
actualisationnote: Should appear at the same time the treaty is laid. Will make the objection period redundant.
datenote: Date of statement

label: Ministerial statement: treaty should nonetheless be ratified (House of Commons)
name: Ministerial statement: treaty should nonetheless be ratified
description: If either House approves a motion that a treaty should not be ratified then the Government can issue a statement stating that the treaty should be ratified regardless of the House's decision. This would trigger objection period B to start in the House of Commons.
actualisationnote: Will likely follow the Commons rejection of a treaty (which does not stop the treaty dead like it does for statutory instruments)
datenote: Date of the statement

label: Ministerial statement: treaty should nonetheless be ratified (House of Lords)
name: Ministerial statement: treaty should nonetheless be ratified
description: If either House approves a motion that a treaty should not be ratified then the Government can issue a statement stating that the treaty should be ratified regardless of the House's decision. This would trigger objection period B to start in the House of Commons.
actualisationnote: Will likely follow the Lords rejection of a treaty (which does not stop the treaty dead like it does for statutory instruments)
datenote: Date of the statement

label: Ministerial statement laid before the House of Commons: objection period A extended (House of Commons)
name: Ministerial statement laid before the House of Commons: objection period A extended
description: Government can issue a statement if they wish to extend objection period A of a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010.  The statement can be laid at any point during objection period A as long as a motion to not ratify the treaty has not been approved in either House.  The objection period can also be extended multiple times.
actualisationnote: If this happens, the "period a ends" step is updated with the new date, and not actualised once more.
linknote: Gov.uk link if available otherwise Commons V&P anchor link.
datenote: Date of statement

label: Ministerial statement laid before the House of Lords: objection period A extended (House of Lords)
name: Ministerial statement laid before the House of Lords: objection period A extended
description: Government can issue a statement if they wish to extend the objection period A of a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010.  The statement can be laid at any point during objection period A as long as a motion to not ratify the treaty has not been approved in either House.  The objection period can also be extended multiple times.
actualisationnote: If this happens, the "period a ends" step is updated with the new date, and not actualised once more.
linknote: Gov.uk if link is available, otherwise Lords business paper link
datenote: Date of statement laid

label: Objection period A ends
name: Objection period A ends
description: The time available for both Houses to object to the treaty, if they wish. Usually 21 days from when the treaty is laid but the Government can issue a statement extending the period. During the objection period treaties can be withdrawn, while procedure concludes at that point it does not affect the objection period end date.
actualisationnote: Usually 21 days after treaty laid (please see scrutiny tabled) but can be extended by Govt or by agreed motion in the Lords.
datenote: See the scrutiny table for date

label: Objection period B ends
name: Objection period B ends
description: If the House of Commons resolves, within period A, that a treaty should not be ratified and a Minister has laid before Parliament a statement indicating the opinion that the treaty should  nevertheless be ratified then objection period B begins. Objection period B is the time available, 21 days, for the House of Commons to object to the treaty again, if they wish. Objection period B can occur multiple times.
actualisationnote: Actualise with a future date when clock starts.
datenote: See the scrutiny table

label: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified approved (House of Commons)
name: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified approved
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. A motion to not ratify, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, could delay ratification due to a second period (Period B) of scrutiny starting in the House of Commons.
actualisationnote: This will usually follow a debate and the question being put. Note that this does not conclude the procedure like a similar motion in the statutory instrument procedures would, the likelihood is a following ministerial statement.
datenote: Date of vote

label: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified approved (House of Lords)
name: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified approved
description: In the House of Lords, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. The motion, if approved or rejected, does not stop ratification of the treaty but the Government must provide a statement setting out why the treaty should be nonetheless ratified.
actualisationnote: This will usually follow a debate and the question being put. Note that this does not conclude the procedure like a similar motion in the statutory instrument procedures would, the likelihood is a following ministerial statement.
datenote: Date of Lords minutes

label: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified rejected (House of Commons)
name: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified rejected
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. A motion to not ratify, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, could delay ratification due to a second period (Period B) of scrutiny starting in the House of Commons.
actualisationnote: Will follow a debate in the Commons and will be accompanied by the question step
datenote: Date of the vote

label: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified rejected (House of Lords)
name: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified rejected
description: In the House of Lords, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. The motion, if approved or rejected, does not stop ratification of the treaty but the Government must provide a statement setting out why the treaty should be nonetheless ratified.
actualisationnote: Will follow a debate in the Lords and will be accompanied by the question step
datenote: Lords Minute date

label: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified tabled (House of Commons)
name: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified tabled
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. A motion to not ratify, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, could delay ratification due to a second period (Period B) of scrutiny starting in the House of Commons.
actualisationnote: These can be tabled at any time during objection periods A or B. Debates will usually be scheduled as a result of the tabling.
datenote: Date of the EDM

label: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified tabled (House of Lords)
name: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified tabled
description: In the House of Lords, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. The motion, if approved or rejected, does not stop ratification of the treaty but the Government must provide a statement setting out why the treaty should be nonetheless ratified.
actualisationnote: These can be tabled at any time during objection period A. Debates will usually be scheduled as a result of the tabling.
linknote: Lords prayer in the LBP (Individual link) or PDF if not quick enough
datenote: Date the prayer first appeared in the LBP

label: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified withdrawn (House of Commons)
name: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified withdrawn
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. A motion to not ratify, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, could delay ratification due to a second period (Period B) of scrutiny starting in the House of Commons.
actualisationnote: Motions can be withdrawn at anytime before they're debated but they can also be withdrawn at the end of the debate. It is worth keeping an eye on the EDM
linknote: Either the EDM itself if withdrawn before debate or V&P anchor if withdrawn post debate
datenote: Date of the Vote/EDM.

label: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified withdrawn (House of Lords)
name: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified withdrawn
description: In the House of Lords, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. The motion, if approved or rejected, does not stop ratification of the treaty but the Government must provide a statement setting out why the treaty should be nonetheless ratified. The motion can be withdrawn at any point between tabling and the Question being put.
actualisationnote: Motions can be withdrawn at anytime before they're debated but they can also be withdrawn at the end of the debate. It is worth keeping an eye on the LBP to make sure the motion hasn't disappeared
linknote: Link depends on the nature of withdrawal, if withdrawn before the debate there is no link, if withdrawn after the debate then the LBP minute.
datenote: Date of Lords Minute/disappearance of motion from LBP

label: Treaty signed
name: Treaty signed
description: This shows the text of the treaty. This step is not part of parliamentary procedure, it is an indicative starting step for the purposes of the Treaty Tracker website.
actualisationnote: Starts off the treaty procedure
datenote: Date on the treaty itself

label: Treaty withdrawn from the House of Commons (House of Commons)
name: Treaty withdrawn from the House of Commons
description: Government has withdrawn the treaty from the House of Commons and therefore the procedure relating to that treaty has concluded.
actualisationnote: This step concludes the procedure.
datenote: Date of the V&P (or stated in the V&P)

label: Non-fatal amendment to legislative consent motion tabled (Senedd Cymru)
name: Non-fatal amendment to legislative consent motion tabled

label: Treaty withdrawn from the House of Lords (House of Lords)
name: Treaty withdrawn from the House of Lords
description: Government has withdrawn the treaty from the House of Lords and therefore procedure has concluded.
actualisationnote: This step concludes the procedure.
datenote: Date of the Lords Papers

label: Parliamentary scrutiny concluded, Government cannot ratify treaty (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Parliamentary scrutiny concluded, Government cannot ratify treaty
description: Parliamentary scrutiny has concluded for this treaty and Government cannot proceed to ratification. It is unlikely that any further scrutiny will occur.
actualisationnote: Can only occur if the Government withdraws the treaty
datenote: Date treaty has been withdrawn by Government

label: Parliamentary scrutiny concluded, Government can ratify treaty (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Parliamentary scrutiny concluded, Government can ratify treaty
description: Parliamentary scrutiny has concluded for this treaty and Government can now proceed to ratification within any other conditions that may or may not be required. It is unlikely that any further parliamentary scrutiny will occur.
actualisationnote: Follows the end of objection period A or B if no other business has occurred during that clock period. Or follows a motion tabled to stop the Government ratifying has been rejected.
datenote: End of objection period A or B or when a motion has been rejected.

label: Information paragraph provided by the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee (SLSC) (House of Lords)
name: Information paragraph provided by the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee (SLSC)
description: Instrument considered by the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee which raised no concerns but felt the instrument substantive enough it warranted an information paragraph.
actualisationnote: Instruments noted as of interest will appear under the report heading "Instruments of Interest" We will first see notice of the report in the Vote/LBP on a Wednesday but the report will be published on a Thursday so we actualise retrospectively. If the SLSC includes a previously considered instrument in another report with a different outcome please check with Jane White. While this is possible it is not considered usual practice and must be checked before we actualise any further steps.
linknote: SLSC Report - anchor link to specific SI
datenote: Date the SLSC met (usually a Tuesday or Wednesday depending on which Sub-Comm).

label: Considered by the International Trade Committee (ITC) (House of Commons)
name: Considered by the International Trade Committee (ITC)
description: Most inquiries result in a report to the House, containing recommendations to the department, the wider Government, and sometimes other organisations such as arms-length agencies or regulators.
actualisationnote: Treaties can be considered by multiple select committees with different interests
datenote: Date the committee met rather than the date the committee published the report

label: International Trade Committee (ITC) recommended the treaty should not be ratified (House of Commons)
name: International Trade Committee (ITC) recommended the treaty should not be ratified
description: The International Trade Committee (ITC) can make a number of recommendations to Parliament about a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 including, but not limited to, that the treaty should not be ratified, that a debate be scheduled or that the objection period should be extended.
actualisationnote: Any Commons select committee can consider a treaty and make recommendations
datenote: Date select committee met, not date of report publishing

label: International Trade Committee (ITC) recommended a general debate (House of Commons)
name: International Trade Committee (ITC) recommended a general debate
description: The International Trade Committee (ITC) can make a number of recommendations to Parliament about a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 including, but not limited to, that the treaty should not be ratified, that a debate be scheduled or that the objection period should be extended.
actualisationnote: Suspect this will come from the report itself
datenote: Date select committee met, not date of report publishing

label: General debate (House of Commons)
name: General debate
description: The House of Commons can hold a general debate to discuss a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010. The formal outcome of a general debate has no procedural implications for the treaty..
actualisationnote: Actualise this step when it is scheduled on the Parliamentary Calendar or Future Business so it appears in 'Scheduled upcoming stages' on the workpackage page. The link will need to be updated once the debate has taken place. If it's rescheduled, change the date. Note that debates can take place as an adjournment or backbench debate in the Chamber or as a Westminster Hall debate.
datenote: Date of debate

label: Ministerial statement announcing the publication of the draft (House of Lords)
name: Ministerial statement announcing the publication of the draft
description: A ministerial statement may be made, usually as a written statement, to Parliament when a published draft under Paragraph 14 of Schedule 8 to the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 is made available.
actualisationnote: A written statement might appear at the time a notification of a published draft has been laid before both Houses. It might only appear in one House.
datenote: Date the written statement was made

label: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A tabled (House of Lords)
name: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A tabled
description: In the House of Lords, Members can table a motion to extend objection period A. A motion to extend, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, does not have to be actioned or acknowledged by the Government.
actualisationnote: These motions can be tabled at any stage during objection period A and could lead to a debate and question.
linknote: LBP (if not quick enough to get the html link then the PDF)
datenote: Date the motion was tabled

label: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law lapsed at end of session (House of Lords)
name: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law lapsed at end of session
description: At the end of a Parliamentary session all outstanding motions lapse which means the question will not be put before the House. Government and Members would need to table the motions again in the new session if they wanted to see a resolution.
actualisationnote: All motions lapse at the end of session.
linknote: LBP or if Parliament is dissolved then the proclamation that appears in the Gazette.
datenote: Prorogation/dissolution date

label: Allocated to the EU Select Committee (House of Lords)
name: Allocated to the EU Select Committee
description: Until 2020 treaties could be allocated to any of the House of Lords European Select Committee sub-committees or the wider committee as a whole for consideration within their terms of reference.
actualisationnote: EU Committee comprises the EU Select Committee and six topic focused sub-committees. The EU Select Committee oversees the work of the sub-committees. These seven committees may consider, however the 6 sub-committees do not have the power to report - reports are made in the name of EU Committee.
datenote: Usually the day after the treaty is laid

label: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument not moved (House of Lords)
name: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument not moved
description: A non-fatal motion does not stop a statutory instrument being law but may be used by either House to indicate concern. The most common non-fatal motion in the Lords is a motion to regret. The motion usually gives specific reasons for the regret. Even if agreed, the motion cannot stop or amend the statutory instrument, but gives members an opportunity to put on record their dissent.
actualisationnote: Usually occurs at the end of a debate considering several motions in one, the first will be decided while the others, where necessary, not moved
datenote: Date the motion was not moved

label: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument being law not moved (House of Commons)
name: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument being law not moved
description: A statutory instrument laid under the made negative procedure becomes law on the day the Minister signs it and automatically remains law unless a motion – or ‘prayer’ – to reject it is agreed by either House within 40 sitting days of the instrument being laid.  In the Commons, such motions are usually tabled as an Early Day Motion.
actualisationnote: Usually occurs at the end of a debate considering several motions in one, the first will be decided while the others, where necessary, not moved
datenote: Date the motion was not moved

name: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion not selected by the Presiding Officer

label: Non-fatal motion to object to the treaty not moved (House of Lords)
name: Non-fatal motion to object to the treaty not moved
description: A non-fatal motion does not stop a treaty being ratified but may be used by the House of Lords to show concern. The most common non-fatal motion is a motion to regret. The motion usually gives specific reasons for the regret. Even if agreed, the motion cannot stop or amend the treaty, but gives members an opportunity to put on record their dissent.
actualisationnote: Usually follows a debate that has grouped several treaties into one and will lead with the main motion.
linknote: Lords minutes or Hansard link (dependent on what it is shown in the Lords minutes)
datenote: Date of Lords minute or Hansard

label: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified not moved (House of Lords)
name: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified not moved
description: In the House of Lords, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. The motion, if approved or rejected, does not stop ratification of the treaty but the Government must provide a statement setting out why the treaty should be nonetheless ratified.
actualisationnote: Usually follows a debate that bundles several treaties in together
datenote: Date of Hansard

label: Motion to take note of the instrument tabled (House of Lords)
name: Motion to take note of the instrument tabled
description: The House of Lords provides for debates to take note of a particular issue, without coming to a decision. The motion for the debate can be on any subject, but the phrasing of the motion itself must be neutral in terms.
actualisationnote: Note that motions to take note do not have outcome after debate
linknote: LBP - usually the pdf as the link does not remain active
datenote: Date of tabled - not the day it appears in the LBP, usually the previous sitting day to this.

name: Non-fatal amendment to legislative consent motion not selected by Presiding Officer

label: Motion to take note of the instrument withdrawn (House of Lords)
name: Motion to take note of the instrument withdrawn
description: The House of Lords provides for debates to take note of a particular issue, without coming to a decision. The motion for the debate can be on any subject, but the phrasing of the motion itself must be neutral in terms.
actualisationnote: Motions can be withdrawn at any time after tabling but will usually occur when a number of SIs have been debated together. One SI will be decided while the others will not be moved.
linknote: LBP minute or no link if withdrawn before debate
datenote: Date of the minutes or date of disappearance.

label: Decision on motion (prayer) to stop the instrument becoming law deferred (House of Commons)
name: Decision on motion (prayer) to stop the instrument becoming law deferred
description: After the moment of interruption (the time at which the main business must usually finish), any division on a statutory instrument motion is deferred until the following Wednesday.
actualisationnote: Will usually appear in the Vote with "The Deputy Speaker’s opinion as to the decision of the Question was challenged."
datenote: Date the question was put

label: Communication declaring the instrument had come into operation before being laid before Parliament (House of Commons)
name: Communication declaring the instrument had come into operation before being laid before Parliament
description: The Government must notify the Speaker to the fact that copies of any statutory instrument have yet to be laid before Parliament, and explaining why such copies have not been so laid before the instrument came into operation.
actualisationnote: It is important to keep an eye out for these types of instruments being laid as we would normally expect a communication to appear in the Vote anywhere between 1 day to 3 weeks later.

label: Statement deposited by the Government, declaring the introduction of one or more criminal offences under Schedule 7 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 (House of Lords)
name: Statement deposited by the Government, declaring the introduction of one or more criminal offences under Schedule 7 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018
description: Where an instrument or draft creates a criminal offence, the relevant Minister must make a statement which must include an explanation of why there are good reasons for creating the offence and for the penalty provided in respect of it.
actualisationnote: If this appears create a whole new workpackage using the information provided in the statement.
linknote: The PDF/Word Document from data.parliament. Attached to the deposited paper from Parliamentary Search
datenote: Date of deposit. not date of commitment.

label: Instrument created under paragraph 5(3) of Schedule 7 to the Local Government Finance Act 1988
name: Instrument created under paragraph 5(3) of Schedule 7 to the Local Government Finance Act 1988
description: This shows the text of the instrument. This step is not part of parliamentary procedure, it is an indicative starting step for the purposes of the Statutory Instrument Service website.
actualisationnote: Government must lay an order stating the non-domestic rates for the year. The procedure is slightly different to the normal made affirmative procedure, this step sets out the starting point.
datenote: Made date

label: Local Government Finance Report (England) approved (House of Commons)
name: Local Government Finance Report (England) approved
description: Local Government Finance Report for England has been approved by the House of Commons.
actualisationnote: Government must lay an order stating the non-domestic rates for the year. The procedure is slightly different to the normal made affirmative procedure, this step sets out one of the two causes of approval period ending. The clock period does not follow the usual 28/40 day count rules, instead the end date is based on the approval of the Local Government Finance (England) report or before the 1st March, whichever is first.
linknote: Vote anchor link of the approval
datenote: Date of Vote

label: International Trade Committee (ITC) recommended the treaty should be ratified (House of Commons)
name: International Trade Committee (ITC) recommended the treaty should be ratified
description: The International Trade Committee (ITC) can make a number of recommendations to Parliament about a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 including, but not limited to, that the treaty should not be ratified, that a debate be scheduled or that the objection period should be extended.
actualisationnote: Any Commons select committee can consider a treaty and make recommendations
datenote: Date committee met, not published.

label: Considered by the Joint Committee on Human Rights (JCHR) (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Considered by the Joint Committee on Human Rights (JCHR)
description: Consideration completed by the Joint Committee on Human Rights (JCHR) within their terms of reference.
actualisationnote: JCHR will consider treaties if they are likely to impact human rights.
datenote: Date the committee met, not the date the report was published

label: Question on motion to approve the Local Government Finance Report (England) put (House of Commons)
name: Question on motion to approve the Local Government Finance Report (England) put
description: Ministers must table a motion to approve the Local Government Finance Report (England) in the House of Commons.  Once the instrument has been moved or debated the Question can then be put.
actualisationnote: Made affirmative instruments laid under the Local Government Finance Act 1988 approval period is dependent on the Local Government Finance Report (England) being approved. The question and outcome should be actualised together
datenote: Date of vote

label: Local Government Finance Report (England) rejected (House of Commons)
name: Local Government Finance Report (England) rejected
description: Local Government Finance Report for England has been rejected by the House of Commons.
actualisationnote: Made affirmative instruments laid under the Local Government Finance Act 1988 approval period is dependent on the Local Government Finance Report (England) being approved. If it's rejected we would actualise the 'Instrument stops being law' and 'Procedure concluded' steps.

label: Joint Committee on Human Rights (JCHR) recommended that objection period A be extended (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Joint Committee on Human Rights (JCHR) recommended that objection period A be extended
description: The Joint Committee on Human Rights (JCHR) can make a number of recommendations to Parliament about a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 including, but not limited to, that the treaty should not be ratified, that debates should be scheduled in both Houses or that the objection period should be extended.
actualisationnote: JCHR may make several recommendations on a treaty to the Houses but it is up to the Members/Lords to take up any of the recommendations.
datenote: Date committee met, not the date the report was published

label: Procedure determination period ends
name: Procedure determination period ends
description: A committee in the House of Commons and a committee in the House of Lords examines all legislative reform orders. They consider whether the minister has recommended the appropriate level of scrutiny and can require a higher level. Both committees have 30 days to do this. The period of 30 days begins with the day on which the draft order was laid before Parliament
actualisationnote: The first 30 days applies for all procedures in the wider LRO procedure.
datenote: Refer to egg-timer for end date.

label: International Trade Committee (ITC) recommended that objection period A be extended (House of Commons)
name: International Trade Committee (ITC) recommended that objection period A be extended
description: The International Trade Committee (ITC) can make a number of recommendations to Parliament about a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 including, but not limited to, that the treaty should not be ratified, that a debate be scheduled or that the objection period should be extended.
actualisationnote: The International Trade Committee may make several recommendations on a treaty to the Houses but it is up to the Members to take up any of the recommendations.
datenote: Date the committee met, not the date the report was published

actualisationnote: These motions can be tabled at any stage during objection period A and could lead to a debate and question.

label: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A not moved (House of Commons)
name: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A not moved
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to extend objection period A. A motion to extend, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, does not have to be actioned or acknowledged by the Government.
actualisationnote: This usually occurs when several motions have been debated in one debate under the title of the first motion, usually the first motion is withdrawn so the others are not moved

name: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A lapsed at end of session
description: At the end of a Parliamentary session all outstanding motions lapse which means the questions in those motions will not be put before the House. Government and Members would need to table the motions again in the new session if they wanted to see a resolution.
actualisationnote: All active motions lapse at the end of a session
linknote: Vote or if Parliament is dissolved then the proclamation that appears in the Gazette.

label: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A withdrawn (House of Commons)
name: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A withdrawn
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to extend objection period A. A motion to extend, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, does not have to be actioned or acknowledged by the Government.
actualisationnote: Will usually follow a debate, but not exclusively as motions can be withdrawn at any point after tabling and before the question is put.
linknote: Vote anchor link if following a debate. If withdrawn before debate then no link as the EDM page will disappear.
datenote: Withdrawn date

label: Question on motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A put (House of Commons)
name: Question on motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A put
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to extend objection period A. A motion to extend, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, does not have to be actioned or acknowledged by the Government.
actualisationnote: Will occur at the end of a debate. The debate could include several motions so be careful to check the outcome in the minutes before actualising.
datenote: Date the question was asked/debate took place

label: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A approved (House of Commons)
name: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A approved
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to extend objection period A. A motion to extend, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, does not have to be actioned or acknowledged by the Government.
actualisationnote: Will usually follow a debate and the question being put. This step will also mean that the objection period A end step could change date depending on the specification in the motion or by Government
datenote: Date question asked/debate

name: Petition on legislative consent motion confirmed on confirmation day

label: International Trade Committee (ITC) inquiry opened (House of Commons)
name: International Trade Committee (ITC) inquiry opened
description: Most select committees do most of their work through inquiries into topics within their remit. This could be an area of policy, a draft bill, or a response to an event. Suggestions for inquiry subjects come from many sources, including the chair and committee members, interest groups and members of the public.
actualisationnote: An inquiry into a treaty could happen at any point after the instrument has been laid, and in some rare cases, before it's been laid if the committee has been given prior notice of a treaty from Government. Please check the committee inquiries page for new inquiries.
datenote: Date will be found on the main committee website's inquiries page - https://committees.parliament.uk/inquiries/

label: Environment, Food and Rural Affairs Committee inquiry opened (House of Commons)
name: Environment, Food and Rural Affairs Committee inquiry opened
description: Most select committees do most of their work through inquiries into topics within their remit. This could be an area of policy, a draft bill, or a response to an event. Suggestions for inquiry subjects come from many sources, including the chair and committee members, interest groups and members of the public.
actualisationnote: An inquiry into a treaty could happen at any point after the instrument has been laid, and in some rare cases, before it's been laid if the committee has been given prior notice of a treaty from Government. Please check the committee inquiries page for new inquiries.
datenote: Date will be found on the main committee website's inquiries page - https://committees.parliament.uk/inquiries/

label: Affirmative pre-approval period ends
name: Affirmative pre-approval period ends
description: A draft legislative reform order under the affirmative procedure must lay before the House for 40 days. During that time the relevant committees can take evidence and report their findings on the instrument. At the end of the 40-day period, the draft order can be approved by a resolution of each House of Parliament.
actualisationnote: Note that debates and questions cannot be put on an affirmative LRO until after the clock has ended.
datenote: Refer to egg-timer for end date.

label: Government response to the decision made by the International Trade Committee (ITC) (House of Commons)
name: Government response to the decision made by the International Trade Committee (ITC)
description: The Government are expected to reply to the recommendations in a select committee report within two months of publication.
actualisationnote: Government responses will either be published as a special report by the select committee itself or laid as a command paper.
linknote: If a command paper link to the page on gov.uk. If a special report by the select committee link to the html version.
datenote: If a command paper use date of laying. If a special report by the select committee use date of the V&P.

label: Considered by the Environment, Food and Rural Affairs Committee (House of Commons)
name: Considered by the Environment, Food and Rural Affairs Committee
description: Most inquiries result in a report to the House, containing recommendations to the department, the wider Government, and sometimes other organisations such as arms-length agencies or regulators.
actualisationnote: Treaties can be considered by multiple select committees with different interests
datenote: Date the committee met rather than the date the committee published the report

label: Considered by the International Agreements Committee (IAC) (House of Lords)
name: Considered by the International Agreements Committee (IAC)
description: International Agreements Committee (IAC) is responsible, as of January 2021, for considering treaties subject to CRaG laid before the House. The committee can either draw special attention to a treaty or report it for information.
actualisationnote: International Agreements Committee (IAC) is responsible, as of January 2021, for considering treaties subject to CRaG laid before the House. The committee can either draw special attention to a treaty or report it for information.
datenote: Date the committee met

label: Call for evidence opened by the International Agreements Committee (IAC) (House of Lords)
name: Call for evidence opened by the International Agreements Committee (IAC)
description: Select committee choose topics to investigate and then make recommendations for improvement. As part of this they collect evidence from interested parties to help them understand what's happening and what changes might be needed. Evidence can be in the form of written or oral evidence.
actualisationnote: International Agreements Committee (IAC) can open a call for evidence into a treaty at any point after it has laid, and in a few rare cases before it is laid. Please check the Committee's non-scrutiny page regularly for updates.
datenote: Date specified on the committee's non-scrutiny page

label: Notification from Department announcing the publication of the draft laid before the House of Commons (House of Commons)
name: Notification from Department announcing the publication of the draft laid before the House of Commons
description: After the UK has left the European Union, a two-stage affirmative procedure applies to any instruments that amend or revoke secondary legislation originally made under the European Communities Act 1972 (most Directives were implemented that way). First the statutory instrument must be published in draft for 28 days to allow for comment. Parliament must be notified when the published draft is available, this takes place in the form of a laid letter.
actualisationnote: Notification will appear in the Vote under 'Other Papers' in the Papers Laid section.
linknote: Anchor link of the paper in the Vote
datenote: Date of laying.

label: Notification from Department announcing the publication of the draft laid before the House of Lords (House of Lords)
name: Notification from Department announcing the publication of the draft laid before the House of Lords
description: After the UK has left the European Union, a two-stage affirmative procedure applies to any instruments that amend or revoke secondary legislation originally made under the European Communities Act 1972 (most Directives were implemented that way). First the statutory instrument must be published in draft for 28 days to allow for comment. Parliament must be notified when the published draft is available, this takes place in the form of a laid letter.
actualisationnote: Notification will appear in the Lords Papers as a Command Paper.
datenote: Date the letter is laid.

A motion not having been called is when the motion is on the Order Paper for that day but did not occur. It may or may not be called on a future date.
actualisationnote: A motion not being called will usually mean it is on the Order Paper to be debated or moved (either alone or en bloc with similar motions) but for whatever reason the motion has not been called at all. label: Motion for leave agreed to (House of Commons)
name: Motion for leave agreed to
description: Under the terms of Standing Order No 23, on Tuesdays and Wednesdays one private Member is able to set down a notice of motion for consideration at the commencement of public business for leave to bring in a bill. The Member who makes the motion and a Member who opposes it are each allowed to make a brief explanatory statement, by convention no more than ten minutes each. Such bills are therefore known colloquially as ‘Ten-minute Rule Bills’. If a motion for leave is agreed then the Bill proceeds to have its first reading.

In the Commons, such motions are usually tabled as an Early Day Motion.

label: Written statement invoking the urgent procedure set out in Paragraph 14(6) of Schedule 8 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 (House of Commons)
name: Written statement invoking the urgent procedure set out in Paragraph 14(6) of Schedule 8 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018
description: Paragraph 14 of Schedule 8 of the EU Withdrawal Act 2018 sets out an enhanced procedure whereby a published draft of an instrument must be published 28 days before the instrument is laid before Parliament. Under (6) there is an allowance for Government to bypass this procedure for urgency and lay an instrument. They must provide a written statement to Parliament to inform them of why they have bypassed the procedure.
actualisationnote: The written statement will appear on the day of laying. It will appear in the House of Commons and duplicated in the House of Lords.
datenote: Date of written statement

label: Government response to the decision made by the European Affairs Committee (EAC) (House of Lords)
name: Government response to the decision made by the European Affairs Committee (EAC)
description: The Government has undertaken to respond to most European Affairs Committee reports within two months, although there are exceptions to this rule and there is no obligation for the Government to respond.
actualisationnote: There is no requirement for Government to respond to a committee report but they still could. This will be published by the committee as correspondence, their government response webpage should be checked regularly.
linknote: Html but pdf if no html is available
datenote: Date of correspondence

label: Reasoned amendment on second reading motion approved (House of Commons)
name: Reasoned amendment on second reading motion approved
description: A Member who wishes to place on record any special reasons for not agreeing to the second reading of a bill may move what is known as a ‘reasoned amendment’ to the question for the second reading. If the reasoned amendment is approved it is fatal to the Bill.

label: Ministerial statement summarising representations on the draft order (House of Lords)
name: Ministerial statement summarising representations on the draft order
description: At the end of the 60-day period of a super-affirmative legislative reform order, the minister must make a statement summarising any representations made during the 60 days.
actualisationnote: This could appear either as a written statement in both Houses or as a ministerial statement in the Chamber (repeated in the other House).
linknote: Written statement html or Hansard if a ministerial statement in the Chamber.
datenote: Date statement made

label: Call for evidence opened by the European Affairs Committee (EAC) (House of Lords)
name: Call for evidence opened by the European Affairs Committee (EAC)
description: Select committee choose topics to investigate and then make recommendations for improvement. As part of this they collect evidence from interested parties to help them understand what's happening and what changes might be needed. Evidence can be in the form of written or oral evidence.
actualisationnote: European Affairs Committee (EAC) can open a call for evidence into a treaty at any point after it has laid, and in a few rare cases before it is laid. Please check the Committee's non-scrutiny page regularly for updates.
datenote: Date specified on the committee's non-scrutiny page

label: European Affairs Committee (EAC) inquiry opened (House of Lords)

actualisationnote: The European Affairs Committee (EAC) can open an inquiry into a treaty at any stage after it has been laid or in some rare cases before it is laid. Please check the committee page regularly for any new inquiries.

actualisationnote: The IAC can call for a debate on any treaty regardless of whether it's reported or drawn to the special attention. In order to find the decision you can either check the conclusion of the report or use Ctrl+F and search for the word Debate.
linknote: Use the anchor link closest to the call for the debate
datenote: Date the committee met, not the date the report was published.

label: Question put that the bill be now read a third time (House of Commons)
name: Question put that the bill be now read a third time
description: When debate on third reading has concluded, or when the time available for debate under the terms of a programme order has been exhausted, the Speaker puts the question already proposed from the Chair. If the question is on an amendment to the question ‘That the bill be now read the third time’, and it is decided that the word ‘now’ stands part of the question, the Speaker declares forthwith that the bill has been read the third time. If the question is on an amendment to leave out all the words after ‘That’ and insert other words, and that is negatived, the Speaker puts the main question forthwith.

description: Remedial orders are a type of statutory instrument. Under the Human Rights Act 1998, courts can declare Acts of Parliament incompatible with the European convention on human rights. The Government can use remedial orders to amend Acts to remove the incompatibility. A minister formally presents (or ‘lays’) a proposal for a draft remedial order. Parliament has 60 days to consider the proposal.

label: Minister recommended the instrument follow the negative procedure (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Minister recommended the instrument follow the negative procedure
description: The way legislative reform orders are scrutinised is not fixed. When ministers lay a draft legislative reform order, they propose what level of scrutiny it should face. Under the negative procedure, the legislative reform order is presented in draft and is made unless either House objects to it within 40 days.
actualisationnote: This will be part of the laying letter and the explanatory memorandum. It will be obvious to us which procedure has been recommended by what title it falls under Papers Laid in the Vote.
linknote: Explanatory memorandum of the SI on leg.gov.uk
datenote: Date of laying

label: Minister recommended the instrument follow the affirmative procedure
name: Minister recommended the instrument follow the affirmative procedure
description: The way legislative reform orders are scrutinised is not fixed. When ministers lay a draft legislative reform order, they propose what level of scrutiny it should face. Under the affirmative procedure, both Houses have 40 days to consider the draft order and then have to actively approve it before it’s made.
actualisationnote: This will be part of the laying letter and the explanatory memorandum. It will be obvious to us which procedure has been recommended by what title it falls under Papers Laid in the Vote.
linknote: Explanatory memorandum of the SI on leg.gov.uk
datenote: Date of laying

label: Minister recommended the instrument follow the super-affirmative procedure (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Minister recommended the instrument follow the super-affirmative procedure
description: The way legislative reform orders are scrutinised is not fixed. When ministers lay a draft legislative reform order, they propose what level of scrutiny it should face. Under the super-affirmative procedure, both Houses have 60 days to consider a proposal for an order. They can recommend changes to the proposed order. The minister has to have regard to these representations and can then decide whether to proceed with the order in the same or an amended form, and lay it as a draft order. This draft order then has to be approved before it can be signed into law by the minister.
actualisationnote: This will be part of the laying letter and the explanatory memorandum. It will be obvious to us which procedure has been recommended by what title it falls under Papers Laid in the Vote.

description: Laying before the House of Commons is the formal registration of a paper. Papers include, but are not limited to, statutory instruments, proposed negative statutory instruments and treaties.  Most papers are laid before both Houses, while some, to do with financial/tax matters, are laid before the House of Commons only.
actualisationnote: All workpackages require this step. For instruments laid in recess the date laid will be the date laid and not the date of the Vote. Links to the Vote to be added retrospectively when the Vote is published on the return of the House. Check procedure flowcharts to be exact on steps to be actualised alongside.

label: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument tabled (House of Lords)
name: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument tabled
description: A non-fatal motion does not stop a statutory instrument being law but may be used by either House to indicate concern. The most common non-fatal motion in the Lords is a motion to regret. The motion usually gives specific reasons for the regret. Even if agreed, the motion cannot stop or amend the statutory instrument, but gives members an opportunity to put on record their dissent.
actualisationnote: Motion text will refer to "revoke" or "withdraw".
linknote: Lords business paper - under Motions related to delegated legislation
datenote: Date tabled - first appearance in the LBP -1

In the Commons, such motions are usually tabled as an Early Day Motion.
actualisationnote: Motion text will refer to "be not made" or "annul"

Even if agreed, the motion cannot stop or amend the statutory instrument, but gives members an opportunity to put on record their dissent.
actualisationnote: Will usually follow a Chamber debate or as a formal proceeding following a GC debate. It could be grouped with the approval motion question itself.

label: Considered by the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments (JCSI) (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Considered by the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments (JCSI)

actualisationnote: We will first see notice of the report in the Vote/LBP on a Thursday but the report will be published on a Friday so we actualise retrospectively.

datenote: Date the JCSI met (usually a Wednesday).

label: Joint Committee on Human Rights (JCHR) inquiry opened (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Joint Committee on Human Rights (JCHR) inquiry opened
description: Select committees do most of their work through inquiries into topics within their remit. This could be an area of policy, a draft bill, or a response to an event. Suggestions for inquiry subjects come from many sources, including the chair and committee members, interest groups and members of the public.
actualisationnote: An inquiry into a treaty could happen at any point after the instrument has been laid, and in some rare cases, before it's been laid if the committee has been given prior notice of a treaty from Government. Please check the committee inquiries page for new inquiries.
datenote: Date will be found on the main committee website's inquiries page - https://committees.parliament.uk/inquiries/

label: Instrument comes into force as law
name: Instrument comes into force as law
description: Some instruments come into force when made, but most contain express provision bringing them into force at a later date (if no later date is given, the default assumption is that an instrument comes into force at the moment of making). The time when an instrument comes into force is known as its ‘commencement’
actualisationnote: When this gets actualised is dependent on the procedure that the SI is laid under, please check flowcharts before actualising. September 2020 - a discussion took place between IDMS and Daniel Greenberg about made affirmatives laid under the Local Government Finance Act 1988. They are made without a number and are not effective until the Local Government Finance report for England is approved. The discussions led to what 'in effect' and 'into force' mean with Greenberg suggesting they mean different things. After discussion we've established that 'comes into force' is the instruction that makes an instrument effective and therefore ‘comes into force’ is enough for our maps.
datenote: Equals CiF date, if there is one; if CiF is covered by note, no date should be added to the business item

name: Considered by the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee (SLSC)
description: Consideration completed by the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee within their terms of reference.
actualisationnote: We will first see notice of the report in the Vote/LBP on a Wednesday but the report will be published on a Thursday so we actualise retrospectively.
linknote: SLSC report - depends on the outcome of the instrument
datenote: Date the SLSC met (usually a Tuesday or Wednesday depending on which Sub-Comm).

label: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument approved (House of Lords)
name: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument approved
description: A non-fatal motion does not stop a statutory instrument being law but may be used by either House to indicate concern. The most common non-fatal motion in the Lords is a motion to regret. The motion usually gives specific reasons for the regret. Even if agreed, the motion cannot stop or amend the statutory instrument, but gives members an opportunity to put on record their dissent.
actualisationnote: Motion text will refer to "revoke" or "withdraw". Will follow a Lords debate and question being put, it could be grouped with the approval motion of the SI itself.
datenote: Date of the decision

label: Grand Committee debate (House of Lords)
name: Grand Committee debate
description: Debates in a Grand Committee takes place on a motion that "the Committee has considered" the statutory instrument. This is the most common form of debate in the House of Lords for statutory instruments before the question to approve the instrument is made in the Chamber.
actualisationnote: Actualise this step when it is scheduled on the Parliamentary Calendar or Future Business so it appears in 'Scheduled upcoming stages' on the workpackage page. The link will need to be updated once the debate has taken place. If it's rescheduled, change the date.
datenote: Date of debate

label: Question on motion (prayer) to stop the instrument being law put (House of Commons)
name: Question on motion (prayer) to stop the instrument being law put
description: A statutory instrument laid under the made negative procedure becomes law on the day the Minister signs it and automatically remains law unless a motion – or ‘prayer’ – to reject it is agreed by either House within 40 sitting days of the instrument being laid.  In the Commons, such motions are usually tabled as an Early Day Motion.
actualisationnote: Will follow a Commons debate and the decision could also be deferred.
datenote: Date of question

label: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion not moved (Senedd Cymru)
name: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion not moved

label: Chamber debate (House of Commons)
name: Chamber debate
description: Debates in the House of Commons Chamber can take place on different forms of motions in respect to statutory instruments and treaties.
actualisationnote: Actualise this step when it is scheduled on the Parliamentary Calendar or Future Business so it appears in 'Scheduled upcoming stages' on the workpackage page. The link will need to be updated once the debate has taken place. If it's rescheduled, change the date.
datenote: Date of debate

label: Motion to refer to a Delegated Legislation Committee (DLC) (House of Commons)
name: Motion to refer to a Delegated Legislation Committee (DLC)
description: In the case of prayers tabled in the Commons against instruments subject to the negative procedure or motions tabled against treaties, a motion may be made by a Minister to refer the motion to a delegated legislation committee.
actualisationnote: Will appear as a formal proceeding.
datenote: Date of the motion

label: Question on motion to approve the instrument put (House of Commons)
name: Question on motion to approve the instrument put
description: Ministers table motions to approve affirmative instruments in both the House of Commons and House of Lords.  Instruments cannot be debated without the motion being tabled.
actualisationnote: A decision or a deferment could follow the question being asked.
datenote: Date of question being asked - takes into account divisions

label: Motion to approve the instrument withdrawn (House of Lords)
name: Motion to approve the instrument withdrawn

label: Motion to approve the instrument withdrawn (House of Commons)
name: Motion to approve the instrument withdrawn

actualisationnote: This may be in the Commons Future Business by way of omission when it previously existed. If a motion is withdrawn after debate then the Votes will be the source.

name: Non-fatal motion to object to the instrument tabled
