The most common non-fatal motion in the Lords is a motion to regret. The motion usually gives specific reasons for the regret. Even if agreed, the motion cannot stop or amend the statutory instrument, but gives members an opportunity to put on record their dissent.
actualisationnote: Motion text will refer to "revoke" or "withdraw"
datenote: Date tabled. This is not the same as date of  notice (aka appearance in LBP).  Date tabled will be date of notice -1, Tuesday to Thursday. When date of notice falls on Monday, it will be the previous Friday irrespective of whether the previous Friday was non-sitting day. Where Date of Notice is first day back following a recess: date tabled tbc

label: Bill brought from the House of Lords (House of Commons)
name: Bill brought from the House of Lords
description: If the bill started in the House of Lords, after the Lords third reading it goes to the House of Commons for its first reading. The Commons reprints the bill with the Lords amendments.

label: Question on non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument put (House of Lords)
name: Question on non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument put
description: A non-fatal motion does not stop a statutory instrument being law but may be used by either House to indicate concern. The most common non-fatal motion in the Lords is a motion to regret. The motion usually gives specific reasons for the regret. Even if agreed, the motion cannot stop or amend the statutory instrument, but gives members an opportunity to put on record their dissent.
actualisationnote: Will usually follow debate or as a separate motion following a Grand Committee debate
datenote: Date of decision

label: Motion to approve the instrument tabled by the Government (House of Commons)
name: Motion to approve the instrument tabled by the Government
description: Ministers table motions to approve affirmative instruments in both the House of Commons and House of Lords.  Instruments cannot be debated without the motion being tabled.
actualisationnote: This step will only appear once. If you spot it again in the Commons FB then raise with the Journal Office.

Where Date of Notice is first day back following a recess: date tabled tbc

label: Considered for English votes for English laws (EVEL) certification (House of Commons)
name: Considered for English votes for English laws (EVEL) certification
description: The Speaker must examine every statutory instrument which is considered in the Chamber, or in a Delegated Legislation Committee. The EVEL process is designed to ensure that legislation that affects only England, or England and Wales, is approved by a majority of MPs representing English constituencies, or English and Welsh constituencies. It also applies to legislation introducing a tax measure that affects only England, Wales and Northern Ireland, which must be approved by a majority of MPs representing constituencies in those areas.
actualisationnote: Note we may get notification of this before the Certificate appears in the Vote. We actualise and then update our data when it does appear, unless grouped with a Not Certified which we actualise as per JO instructions
linknote: Depends on the outcome - if not certified there will be no link. If certified then the Vote
datenote: Date of consideration if 'not certified' or date the certificate appears in the Vote

In the Commons, such motions are usually tabled as an Early Day Motion.

label: Instrument stops being law
name: Instrument stops being law
description: Applies to made instruments only. Made negative instruments stop being law if either House approves a fatal motion to stop the instrument being law within the 40 day objection period. Made affirmative instruments stop being law if either House fails to approve the instrument within the approval period.
actualisationnote: Actualised according to House decision (only one need say no). Acts as a Parliament instruction to take the instrument out of force.
datenote: Date of decision

label: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument becoming law rejected (House of Lords)
name: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument becoming law rejected
description: A statutory instrument laid under the draft negative procedure cannot be made into law (signed by the minister) if either House approves a motion - or a 'prayer' - to stop the instrument becoming law within 40 sitting days of the instrument being laid.
actualisationnote: Will usually follow a debate and the question being put/deferred
datenote: Date of decision

label: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument becoming law withdrawn (House of Lords)
name: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument becoming law withdrawn
description: A statutory instrument laid under the draft negative procedure cannot be made into law (signed by the minister) if either House approves a motion - or a 'prayer' - to stop the instrument becoming law within 40 sitting days of the instrument being laid.
actualisationnote: A motion can be withdrawn at any point after tabling but usually happens immediately after debate. If withdrawn before debate takes place then the motion will no longer appear in LBP or scheduled.

actualisationnote: Motion text will refer to "be not made" or "Declines to approve" or "annuls".
linknote: LBP individual link or if not quick enough Lords PDF

actualisationnote: Will follow debate on the motion.
datenote: Date of question

label: Approval period ends
name: Approval period ends
description: The time available for a made affirmative instrument to be approved by the House(s). The approval period is defined in legislation as how long the House had to act, often 28 or 40 calendar days, from the day they are made (signed into law). During the approval period instruments can be revoked, while procedure concludes at that point it does not affect the approval period end date.
actualisationnote: Please check with JO what the dates are as they can differ from the norm - normally 28/40 days but can be longer/shorter depending on what the SI states and in some cases these steps are not needed.
datenote: See scope note.

label: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument becoming law approved (House of Lords)
name: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument becoming law approved
description: A statutory instrument laid under the draft negative procedure cannot be made into law (signed by the minister) if either House approves a motion - or a 'prayer' - to stop the instrument becoming law within 40 sitting days of the instrument being laid.
actualisationnote: Will usually follow a debate and question/deferment. Will end the SI procedure.
datenote: Date of decision

label: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument becoming law rejected (House of Commons)
name: Motion (prayer) to stop the instrument becoming law rejected
description: A statutory instrument laid under the draft negative procedure cannot be made into law (signed by the minister) if either House approves a motion - or a 'prayer' - to stop the instrument becoming law within 40 sitting days of the instrument being laid.

label: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument tabled (House of Commons)
name: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument tabled
description: A non-fatal motion (prayer) does not stop a statutory instrument being law but may be used by either House to indicate concern. In the Commons, a prayer motion tabled outside the 40 sitting days period is non-fatal as it can only object to the instrument, rather than stop it.
actualisationnote: Motion text will refer to "revoke" or "withdraw". A non-fatal motion can be tabled as an EDM during a prayer period. In some cases it may be tabled in place of a fatal prayer if the instrument is set to be debated after the clock ends.
linknote: EDM page. If it's a motion that replaces a fatal motion for debate after the clock ends then use the Order Paper/Commons Future Business A
datenote: Date tabled if EDM. -1 sitting date if Order Paper/FBA tabling

actualisationnote: A motion can be withdrawn at any point after tabling but usually happens immediately after debate. Note if the motion is withdrawn before debate it is difficult to keep track of this (keep checking EDM itself)

name: Bill to be printed and ordered to be a read second time
description: Once the Bill has been read the first time without any question being put, it shall be ordered to be read a second time on such day as the Member presenting it shall appoint, and shall be ordered to be printed.

label: Objection period ends
name: Objection period ends
description: The time available for the House(s) to object to a negative instrument if they wish. The objection period is defined in legislation as how long the House had to take action, usually 40 calendar days from when the instrument is laid and the House(s) are not in a recess of more than four days. During the objection period instruments can be withdrawn or revoked, while procedure concludes at that point it does not affect the objection period end date.
actualisationnote: Subject to change depending on Parliamentary recesses/prorogation etc. but will be '40 days from laying"
datenote: Refer to egg-timer

label: European Statutory Instruments Committee (ESIC) agreed the instrument should follow the negative procedure (House of Commons)
name: European Statutory Instruments Committee (ESIC) agreed the instrument should follow the negative procedure
description: The European Statutory Instruments Committee, under the remit of Schedule 7 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, agreed that the appropriate procedure for the instrument is for it to be subject to annulment. The committee was active from July 2018 until the 29 May 2024 when the 58th Parliament was dissolved.
actualisationnote: We will first see notice of the report in the Vote on a Wednesday but the report will be published on a Thursday so we actualise retrospectively. The committee's functions have been replaced by departmental committees.
datenote: Date ESIC met (usually a Tuesday)

label: Oral evidence session by the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee (DPRRC) (House of Lords)
name: Oral evidence session by the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee (DPRRC)
description: The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee has the power to invite Ministers to oral evidence sessions before or after they have considered an instrument.
actualisationnote: The DPRRC can hold an oral evidence session at any point after an LRO has been laid before the House of Lords. Multiple oral evidence sessions can take place.
linknote: Link to transcript. If transcript is unavailable then link to Parliament TV session.
datenote: Date oral evidence took place

label: European Statutory Instruments Committee (ESIC) recommended the instrument should follow the affirmative procedure (House of Commons)
name: European Statutory Instruments Committee (ESIC) recommended the instrument should follow the affirmative procedure
description: The European Statutory Instruments Committee, under the remit of Schedule 7 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, recommended that the appropriate procedure for the instrument is for a draft of it be laid before, and approved by a resolution, of each House before it is made.  The committee was active from July 2018 until the 29 May 2024 when the 58th Parliament was dissolved.
actualisationnote: We will first see notice of the report in the Vote on a Wednesday but the report will be published on a Thursday so we actualise retrospectively. The committee's functions have been replaced by departmental committees.
datenote: Date ESIC met (usually a Tuesday)

name: Certified as England only under the English votes for English laws (EVEL) process
description: The English Votes for English Laws (EVEL) process is designed to ensure that legislation that affects only England, or England and Wales, is approved by a majority of MPs representing English constituencies, or English and Welsh constituencies. It also applies to legislation introducing a tax measure that affects only England, Wales and Northern Ireland, which must be approved by a majority of MPs representing constituencies in those areas.

description: Instrument considered by the Statutory Instrument Select Committee which raised concerns in line with the committee's terms of reference.

We will first see notice of the report in the Vote/LBP on a Thursday but the report will be published on a Friday so we actualise retrospectively. Name of step changed from 'Concerns raised' in March 2020. Name of step changed from 'Reported' in October 2023.
linknote: SCSI report - anchor link to specific instrument
datenote: Date the SCSI met (usually a Wednesday).

name: Not drawn to the special attention of the House by the Select Committee on Statutory Instruments (SCSI)

We will first see notice of the report in the Vote on a Thursday but the report will be published on a Friday so we actualise retrospectively.  Name of step changed from 'No concerns' in March 2020. Name of step changed from 'Not reported' in October 2023.

label: Committee sifting period ends
name: Committee sifting period ends
description: Ten sitting days are allowed for the European Statutory Instrument Committee and the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee (HL) to consider whether the instrument should continue with the negative procedure or be upgraded to the affirmative procedure.  During the sifting period instruments can be withdrawn, while procedure concludes at that point it does not affect the sifting period end date.
actualisationnote: The end date will be 10 days (based on the scrutiny table) from the date of laying.

name: Non-fatal motion (prayer) to object to the instrument approved
description: A non-fatal motion (prayer) does not stop a statutory instrument being law but may be used by either House to indicate concern. In the Commons, a prayer motion tabled outside the 40 sitting days period is non-fatal as it can only object to the instrument, rather than stop it.
actualisationnote: Motion text will refer to "revoke" or "withdraw". Will usually follow a Chamber debate or as a formal proceeding following a DLC debate
datenote: Date of decision

label: Reasoned amendment on second reading motion approved (House of Lords)
name: Reasoned amendment on second reading motion approved

label: Instrument cannot become law
name: Instrument cannot become law
description: Draft negative instruments cannot become law if either the House of Commons or House of Lords approve a motion to stop the instrument becoming law within the 40 day praying period. Draft affirmative instruments cannot become law if either the House of Commons or House of Lords reject a motion to approve the instrument. Neither draft negative instruments or draft affirmative instruments can become law if they are withdrawn.
actualisationnote: Actualised according to House decision (only one need say no)
datenote: Date of decision

label: Ministerial statement of disagreement with sifting recommendation (House of Commons)
name: Ministerial statement of disagreement with sifting recommendation
description: Government must issue a statement if they disagree with the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee or European Statutory Instruments Committee recommendation that a proposed negative statutory instrument be upgraded to the affirmative procedure
actualisationnote: A statement could follow once both/or either ESIC and SLSC have recommended a PNSI for the affirmative procedure but the Govt wishes to continue with the negative procedure
linknote: If written statement link to written statement pages and not Hansard, if ministerial statement on the floor link to Hansard
datenote: Date of statement

label: Ministerial statement of disagreement with sifting recommendation (House of Lords)
name: Ministerial statement of disagreement with sifting recommendation
description: Government must issue a statement if they disagree with the Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee or European Statutory Instruments Committee recommendation that a proposed negative statutory instrument be upgraded to the affirmative procedure
actualisationnote: A statement could follow once both/or either ESIC and SLSC have recommended a PNSI for the affirmative procedure but the Govt wishes to continue with the negative procedure
linknote: If written statement link to written statement pages and not Hansard, if ministerial statement on the floor link to Hansard
datenote: Date of statement

label: Question on motion that the treaty should not be ratified put (House of Commons)
name: Question on motion that the treaty should not be ratified put
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. A motion to not ratify, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, could delay ratification due to a second period (Period B) of scrutiny starting.
actualisationnote: Can occur straight after a Chamber debate or as a separate motion after a DLC debate. The decision could also be deferred.
datenote: Date of the Vote

label: Question on motion that the treaty should not be ratified put (House of Lords)
name: Question on motion that the treaty should not be ratified put
description: In the House of Lords, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. The motion, if approved or rejected, does not stop ratification of the treaty but the Government must provide a statement setting out why the treaty should be nonetheless ratified.
actualisationnote: Occurs straight after debate in the Chamber
datenote: Date of Lords Minutes

label: Ministerial statement under Section 22 of the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010: Section 20 does not apply (House of Commons)
name: Ministerial statement under Section 22 of the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010: Section 20 does not apply
description: Allows a Minister to certify that a treaty can be ratified although it has not met the requirements of section 20 of the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010.  It applies in exceptional circumstances. These are not defined. The only restriction on a Minister using the power under section 22 is if either House has already resolved that the treaty should not be ratified.
actualisationnote: Should appear at the same time the treaty is laid. Will make the objection period redundant.
datenote: Date of statement

label: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion not agreed to (Senedd Cymru)
name: Fatal amendment to legislative consent motion not agreed to

label: Allocated to the EU Justice Sub-Committee (House of Lords)
name: Allocated to the EU Justice Sub-Committee
description: Until 2020 treaties could be allocated to any of the House of Lords European Select Committee sub-committees or the wider committee as a whole for consideration within their terms of reference.
actualisationnote: As of April 2020 this step is unlikely to be actualised. The new International Agreements sub-committee, or the whole committee, will deal exclusively with treaties subject to crag. EU Committee comprises the EU Select Committee and six topic focussed sub-commitees. The EU Select Committee oversees the work of the sub-committees. These seven committees may consider, however the 6 sub-commitees do not have the power to report - reports are made in the name of EU Committee.
datenote: Usually the day after the treaty is laid

label: Motion to take note of the treaty tabled (House of Lords)
name: Motion to take note of the treaty tabled
description: The House of Lords provides for debates to take note of a particular issue, without coming to a decision. The motion for the debate can be on any subject, but the phrasing of the motion itself must be neutral in terms.
actualisationnote: These motions can be tabled at any stage during objection period A. They will always be debated (unless withdrawn) but they won't impact the wider procedure.
linknote: LBP individual link (however if not in time please use Lords pdf)
datenote: Date the motion first appeared in LBP

label: Motion to take note of the treaty withdrawn (House of Lords)
name: Motion to take note of the treaty withdrawn
description: The House of Lords provides for debates to take note of a particular issue, without coming to a decision. The motion for the debate can be on any subject, but the phrasing of the motion itself must be neutral in terms. The motion can be withdrawn at any point between tabling and the Question being put.
actualisationnote: The motion can be withdrawn at any stage before debate or following debate.
linknote: LBP minute or no link if withdrawn before debate
datenote: Date of LBP minutes or disappearance from LBP

label: Decision on motion (prayer) to stop the instrument being law deferred (House of Commons)
name: Decision on motion (prayer) to stop the instrument being law deferred
description: After the moment of interruption (the time at which the main business must usually finish), any division on a statutory instrument motion is deferred until the following Wednesday.
actualisationnote: Will usually appear in the Vote with "The Deputy Speaker’s opinion as to the decision of the Question was challenged."
datenote: Date the question was put

label: Bill brought from the House of Commons (House of Lords)
name: Bill brought from the House of Commons

label: Bill printed (House of Lords)
name: Bill printed

label: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A approved (House of Lords)
name: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A approved
description: In the House of Lords, Members can table a motion to extend objection period A. A motion to extend, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, does not have to be actioned or acknowledged by the Government.
actualisationnote: Will usually follow a debate and the question being put. This step will also mean that the objection period A end step will need to change date depending on the specification in the motion or by Government
datenote: Date question asked/debate

label: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A rejected (House of Lords)
name: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A rejected
description: In the House of Lords, Members can table a motion to extend objection period A. A motion to extend, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, does not have to be actioned or acknowledged by the Government.

datenote: Date the question put/debate

label: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A withdrawn (House of Lords)
name: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A withdrawn
description: In the House of Lords, Members can table a motion to extend objection period A. A motion to extend, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, does not have to be actioned or acknowledged by the Government.

datenote: Date of debate

label: Non-fatal motion to object to the treaty tabled (House of Lords)
name: Non-fatal motion to object to the treaty tabled
description: A non-fatal motion does not stop a treaty being ratified but may be used by the House of Lords to show concern. The most common non-fatal motion is a motion to regret. The motion usually gives specific reasons for the regret. Even if agreed, the motion cannot stop or amend the treaty, but gives members an opportunity to put on record their dissent.
actualisationnote: Usually a 'Motion to regret'
linknote: LBP individual link or pdf if not quick enough to catch the html link
datenote: Date motion first appeared in LBP

label: Question on motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A put (House of Lords)
name: Question on motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A put

actualisationnote: Will occur at the end of a debate. The debate could include several motions so be careful to check the outcome in the minutes before actualising.

description: The Secondary Legislation Scrutiny Committee can publish additional information once they've considered and made a decision on an instrument. The additional information tends, though is not limited, to be in the form of correspondence included in a later report.
actualisationnote: It is possible that the SLSC may publish further information (correspondence or oral evidence) after they have made a decision on an instrument, if this happens please check with Jane White before actualising this step.

name: Allocated to the EU International Agreements Sub-Committee
description: Until 2020 treaties could be allocated to any of the House of Lords European Select Committee sub-committees or the committee as a whole for consideration within their terms of reference. In January 2021 the International Agreements sub-committee was upgraded to a fully-fledged committee in itself.
actualisationnote: Introduced as a new step in April 2020 - from this point forward this sub-committee is responsible for considering all treaties (unless the overall EU Select committee decides to consider). This step does not have the power to report - reports are made in the name of EU Committee.
datenote: Usually the day after the treaty is laid

label: Correspondence published by the EU International Agreements Sub-Committee (House of Lords)
name: Correspondence published by the EU International Agreements Sub-Committee
description: The EU International Agreements Sub-Committee (IAC) can publish any information they see fit in regards to a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010. The information tends, though is not limited, to be in the form of correspondence.
actualisationnote: It is possible for the IAC to publish evidence/correspondence before or after the overall committee reports. Indexers should check the sub-committee's homepage regularly for any such publishing, this will not appear in the Lords Minutes.
linknote: HTML if possible but PDF if it's the only option available
datenote: Date of publishing

label: Information published by the EU Select Committee (House of Lords)
name: Information published by the EU Select Committee
description: The EU Select Committee can publish additional information once they've considered and made a decision on a treaty. The additional information tends, though is not limited, to be in the form of correspondence included in a later report.
actualisationnote: It is possible for the EU Select Committee to publish evidence/correspondence before or after a report on a treaty. Indexers should actively check the committee's publication page as the Lords Minutes will not publish such information.
linknote: HTML if possible but PDF if it's the only option available
datenote: Date of publishing

label: Extended objection period A ends
name: Extended objection period A ends
description: Government can issue a statement if they wish to extend the objection period A of a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010.  The statement can be made at any point during objection period A as long as a motion to not ratify the treaty has not been approved in either House.  The objection period can also be extended multiple times.
actualisationnote: This step should be actualised at the same time as the new 'Extended objection period A begins' step with a future date. This step can be actualised multiple times if the Government decides to lay multiple statements to extend
datenote: Dependent on how many days the Govt stipulates. Please check with Jane White what the date should be.

label: Instrument revoked by another instrument
name: Instrument revoked by another instrument
description: Instruments can be revoked by primary or secondary legislation in full or in part. If revoked in full then they stop being law from that point onwards. Revocation does not prejudice the validity of anything previously done under the instrument.
actualisationnote: This step can be actualised if a made instrument is revoked before parliamentary scrutiny of the instrument has concluded.
datenote: Made date of the revoking instrument

label: Instrument replaced by revoking instrument
name: Instrument replaced by revoking instrument
description: Instruments can be revoked by primary or secondary legislation in full or in part. If revoked in full, the instrument stops being law from that point. Revocation does not prejudice the validity of anything previously done under the instrument.
actualisationnote: This step can be actualised if a revoked instrument has been replaced like for like - in whole - by the revoking instrument. Usually easy to spot in the explanatory memorandum to the revoking instrument. This step is a temporary workaround, pending an update to the legislation model to relate a revoked instrument to a revoking instrument.
linknote: Link to the SI Service page of the new instrument
datenote: Made date of the new instrument

label: Instrument lapsed
name: Instrument lapsed
description: Made affirmative instruments lapse once they reach the end of their approval period and have not been approved by the House(s).
actualisationnote: This step is to be used only on made affirmatives when the clock has ended but the instrument has failed to be approved or rejected. We spot it when the clock ends and we notice nothing else has happened. Usually the Govt allow it to run out. This step has been added for users to make it clear that the instrument cannot proceed any further, if the made affirmative procedure model had logic gates we'd be able to explain why an instrument has ended without approval
datenote: Date the clock ends

description: This shows the text of the instrument. This step is not part of parliamentary procedure, it is an indicative starting step for the purposes of the Statutory Instrument Service website.
actualisationnote: This would be actualised when a statement has been deposited before the House of Commons or bicamerally. We would create a new workpackage with the information provided in the statement and then actualise this as the start step. Instruments with this start step would not receive 'Instrument created'

label: Statement deposited by the Government, declaring the introduction of one or more criminal offences under Schedule 7 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 (House of Commons)
name: Statement deposited by the Government, declaring the introduction of one or more criminal offences under Schedule 7 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018
description: Where an instrument or draft creates a criminal offence, the relevant Minister must make a statement which must include an explanation of why there are good reasons for creating the offence and for the penalty provided in respect of it.
actualisationnote: If this appears create a whole new workpackage using the information provided in the statement.
linknote: The PDF/Word Document from data.parliament. Attached to the deposited paper from Parliamentary Search
datenote: Date of deposit. not date of commitment.

label: Instrument created under Section 55(3) in conjunction with Section 56(5) of the Sanctions and Anti-money Laundering Act 2018
name: Instrument created under Section 55(3) in conjunction with Section 56(5) of the Sanctions and Anti-money Laundering Act 2018
description: This shows the text of the instrument. This step is not part of parliamentary procedure, it is an indicative starting step for the purposes of the Statutory Instrument Service website.
actualisationnote: Only actualised for made affirmative instruments laid under the Sanctions and Anti-money Laundering Act 2018. It replaces the normal 'Instrument created' step. These SIs are identified in an information paragraph at the top of the SI on leg.gov
datenote: Made date

label: Commencement Order made (signed into law) triggering approval period
name: Commencement Order made (signed into law) triggering approval period
description: Made affirmative instruments made under Section 55(3) in conjunction with Section 56(5) of the Sanctions Act are subject to an approval period. The approval period does not begin until the first day on which any provisions of the regulations comes into force. Provisions come into force on any such day that an appropriate Minister considers it appropriate to do so, this is usually done by the making of a commencement order.
actualisationnote: Only applies to made affirmative instruments laid under section 55(3) in conjunction with section 56(5) of the Sanctions and Anti-money Laundering Act 2018. When the FCO make a commencement order the provisions of the regulation come into force and the approval period begins.
datenote: Specified in the commencement order for the individual instrument.

label: Instrument under paragraph 5(3) of Schedule 7 to the Local Government Finance Act 1988 made (signed into law)
name: Instrument under paragraph 5(3) of Schedule 7 to the Local Government Finance Act 1988 made (signed into law)
description: A Statutory Instrument is made when signed by the minister (or person with authority under the Act); in other words, the instrument is not in draft. The Treasury make these instruments in exercise of the powers conferred by paragraph 5(3) of Schedule 7 to the Local Government Finance Act 1988.
actualisationnote: Government must lay an order stating the non-domestic rates for the year. The procedure is slightly different to the normal made affirmative procedure, this step sets out the making point.
datenote: Made date

label: Approval period for instrument under paragraph 5(3) of Schedule 7 to the Local Government Finance Act 1988 ends
name: Approval period for instrument under paragraph 5(3) of Schedule 7 to the Local Government Finance Act 1988 ends
description: Made affirmatives laid under the Local Government Finance Act 1988 must be approved by resolution of the House of Commons before the approval by that House of the local government finance report for the year or before 1 March in the preceding financial year (whichever is earlier).
actualisationnote: Government must lay an order stating the non-domestic rates for the year. The procedure is slightly different to the normal made affirmative procedure, this step sets out the end of the approval period. The clock period does not follow the usual 28/40 day count rules, instead the end date is based on the approval of the Local Government Finance (England) report or before the 1st March, whichever is first.
datenote: Actualise initially with 28/29 February. If the Local Govt Finance Report is approved before this date we would actualise retrospectively.

label: Deadline for local authority budgets
name: Deadline for local authority budgets
description: Made affirmatives laid under the Local Government Finance Act 1988 must be approved by resolution of the House of Commons before the approval by that House of the local government finance report for the year or before 1 March (the deadline for local authority budgets) in the preceding financial year (whichever is earlier).
actualisationnote: Actualise when instrument is made (signed into law) Relevant step for instruments laid under paragraph 5(3) of Schedule 7 of the Local Government Finance Act 1988 - if the Local Government Finance (England) report has not been published by the 1st March then the instrument stops being law.
datenote: 28/29 February

label: Super-affirmative pre-approval period ends
name: Super-affirmative pre-approval period ends
description: A draft legislative reform order under the super-affirmative procedure must lay before the House for 60 days. During that time the relevant committees can take evidence and report their findings on the instrument. Representations can also be made. At the end of the 60-day period, the minister must make a statement stating whether any representations were made and the draft order can be approved by a resolution of each House of Parliament.
actualisationnote: Note that debates and questions cannot be put on a super-affirmative LRO until after the clock has ended.
datenote: Refer to egg-timer for end date.

label: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A not called (House of Commons)
name: Motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A not called
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to extend objection period A. A motion to extend, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, does not have to be actioned or acknowledged by the Government. A motion not having been called is when the motion is on the Order Paper for that day but did not occur. It may or may not be called on a future date.
actualisationnote: A motion not being called will usually mean it is on the Order Paper to be debated or moved (either alone or en bloc with similar motions) but for whatever reason the motion has not been called at all. We notice it by its omission, a comparison of what is due to happen on the Order Paper and what did happen on the Votes & Proceedings.
datenote: Date the motion was due to be called in the House of Commons chamber

label: Decision on motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A deferred (House of Commons)
name: Decision on motion calling on the Government to extend objection period A deferred
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to extend objection period A. A motion to extend, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, does not have to be actioned or acknowledged by the Government.

label: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified not called (House of Commons)
name: Motion that the treaty should not be ratified not called
description: In the House of Commons, Members can table a motion to not ratify a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 procedure. A motion to not ratify, if tabled and passed within 21 days (Period A - subject to extension) of the treaty being laid, could delay ratification due to a second period (Period B) of scrutiny starting. A motion not having been called is when the motion is on the Order Paper for that day but did not occur. It may or may not be called on a future date.
actualisationnote: A motion not being called will usually mean it is on the Order Paper to be debated or moved (either alone or en bloc with similar motions) but for whatever reason the motion has not been called at all. We notice it by its omission, a comparison of what is due to happen on the Order Paper and what did happen on the Votes & Proceedings.

label: Period B starts
name: Period B starts
description: If the Commons passes a resolution that the treaty should not be ratified, a further 21 sitting day period known as Period B is triggered which would prevent the Government from proceeding with ratification.
actualisationnote: This should only be actualised once the Government have provided a statement in response to a Commons resolution that the treaty should not be ratified.
datenote: Date of Government statement or date specified in the statement.

label: Motion for leave set down for consideration (House of Commons)
name: Motion for leave set down for consideration
description: Under the terms of Standing Order No 23, on Tuesdays and Wednesdays one private Member is able to set down a notice of motion for consideration at the commencement of public business for leave to bring in a bill. The Member who makes the motion and a Member who opposes it are each allowed to make a brief explanatory statement, by convention no more than ten minutes each. Such bills are therefore known colloquially as ‘Ten-minute Rule Bills’.

label: Non-fatal motion to object to the treaty not called (House of Lords)
name: Non-fatal motion to object to the treaty not called
description: A non-fatal motion does not stop a treaty being ratified but may be used by the House of Lords to show concern. The most common non-fatal motion is a motion to regret. The motion usually gives specific reasons for the regret. Even if agreed, the motion cannot stop or amend the treaty, but gives members an opportunity to put on record their dissent. A motion not having been called is when the motion is on the Order Paper for that day but did not occur. It may or may not be called on a future date.
actualisationnote: A motion not being called will usually mean it is on the Order Paper to be debated or moved (either alone or en bloc with similar motions) but for whatever reason the motion has not been called at all. label: Instrument created under the urgent procedure set out in Paragraph 14(6) of Schedule 8 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018
name: Instrument created under the urgent procedure set out in Paragraph 14(6) of Schedule 8 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018
description: Paragraph 14 of Schedule 8 of the EU Withdrawal Act 2018 sets out an enhanced procedure whereby a published draft of an instrument must be published 28 days before the instrument is laid before Parliament. Under (6) there is an allowance for Government to bypass this procedure for urgency and lay an instrument.
actualisationnote: This step replaces the standard 'Instrument created' step on an applicable instrument.
datenote: Made date for made instruments, laid date for draft instruments.

name: Considered by the European Affairs Committee (EAC)
description: European Affairs Committee (EAC) is responsible, as of 2021, for considering treaties subject to CRaG laid before the House. They are only likely to report on agreements with the EU or the EEA. The committee can either draw special attention to a treaty or report it for information.
actualisationnote: EAC will report on an adhoc basis and will only look at treaties concerning agreements with the EU or the EEA. The IAC will continue to look at all other committees.

name: Reported for information by the European Affairs Committee (EAC)
description: The European Affairs Committee report treaties under two headings: those to which special attention is drawn, and treaties reported for information only. Treaties in the first category were analysed in detail, whereas brief factual summaries were provided for treaties in the second category.
actualisationnote: European Affairs Committee (EAC) is responsible, as of 2021, for considering treaties subject to CRaG laid before the House. They are only likely to report on agreements with the EU or the EEA. The committee can either draw special attention to a treaty or report it for information.
datenote: Date the committee met

label: Question that the bill be now read a third time agreed (House of Commons)
name: Question that the bill be now read a third time agreed
description: When debate on third reading has concluded, or when the time available for debate under the terms of a programme order has been exhausted, the Speaker puts the question already proposed from the Chair. If the question is on an amendment to the question ‘That the bill be now read the third time’, and it is decided that the word ‘now’ stands part of the question, the Speaker declares forthwith that the bill has been read the third time. If the question is on an amendment to leave out all the words after ‘That’ and insert other words, and that is negatived, the Speaker puts the main question forthwith.

label: Question that the bill be now read a third time rejected (House of Commons)
name: Question that the bill be now read a third time rejected
description: When debate on third reading has concluded, or when the time available for debate under the terms of a programme order has been exhausted, the Speaker puts the question already proposed from the Chair. If the question is on an amendment to the question ‘That the bill be now read the third time’, and it is decided that the word ‘now’ stands part of the question, the Speaker declares forthwith that the bill has been read the third time. If the question is on an amendment to leave out all the words after ‘That’ and insert other words, and that is negatived, the Speaker puts the main question forthwith.

label: Reasoned amendment on third reading motion approved (House of Commons)
name: Reasoned amendment on third reading motion approved
description: A Member who wishes to place on record any special reasons for not agreeing to the third reading of a bill may move what is known as a ‘reasoned amendment’ to the question for the third reading. If the reasoned amendment is approved it is fatal to the Bill.

label: Royal Assent signified (House of Commons)
name: Royal Assent signified
description: The sovereign's assent agreement to a bill passed by both Houses; the final stage of a bill becoming an act of Parliament.

label: Royal Assent not given
name: Royal Assent not given
description: The sovereign's assent agreement to a bill passed by both Houses; the final stage of a bill becoming an act of Parliament. If Royal Assent is not given then the Bill cannot become an Act of Parliament.

label: Royal Assent given
name: Royal Assent given
description: The sovereign's assent agreement to a bill passed by both Houses; the final stage of a bill becoming an act of Parliament.

label: Bill enacted as an Act of Parliament
name: Bill enacted as an Act of Parliament

label: Royal Assent signified (House of Lords)
name: Royal Assent signified

label: Question that the bill be now read a third time agreed (House of Lords)
name: Question that the bill be now read a third time agreed
description: Third reading in the Lords is the chance for members to add any further amendments to a bill, concentrating on making sure the eventual law is effective and workable. If the bill started in the Lords, it goes to the House of Commons for its first reading. The Commons reprints the bill with the Lords amendments. If the bill began in the Commons, it is sent back after third reading in the Lords for consideration of Lords amendments, or, if there have been no amendments in the Lords, is sent to the monarch for royal assent.

label: Reasoned amendment on third reading motion approved (House of Lords)
name: Reasoned amendment on third reading motion approved

actualisationnote: JCHR will consider all proposed draft remedials and the subsequent affirmative remedial orders. They will make a decision on the procedure but may also decide to drawn special attention.

description: Consideration completed by the Joint Committee on Human Rights (JCHR) within their terms of reference. The Committee can make several recommendations from whether the remedial order is appropriate, whether it should be changed or proceed in the same terms. The Committee also makes recommendations on whether the House(s) should approve the order.
actualisationnote: The JCHR meets on an adhoc basis so we should be keeping an eye on the Vote and Lords Minutes for any reports.

description: The Government has undertaken to respond to most Joint Committee on Human Rights reports within two months, although there are exceptions to this rule and there is no obligation for the Government to respond.
actualisationnote: There is no requirement for Government to respond to a JCHR committee report but they still could. This will either be laid as a Command Paper or be published by the Committee as correspondence attached to the original report. Their government response webpage should be checked regularly.
linknote: Html but pdf if no html is available
datenote: Date of laying if a command paper, date of correspondence if a letter.

label: Proposed Remedial Order scrutiny period ends
name: Proposed Remedial Order scrutiny period ends
description: A minister formally presents (or ‘lays’) a proposal for a draft remedial order. Parliament has 60 days to consider the proposal. The Joint Committee on Human Rights (a Committee of Members of the Commons and Lords) also scrutinises the proposal. At the end of the 60-day period, the minister can lay a draft remedial order. The minister has to report details of any representations made during the 60 days and any changes made to the proposal.
actualisationnote: This step should be added to the workpackage at the point of laying with a future date.
datenote: See egg-timer for date.

label: Statement informing the House that a proposal for a draft remedial order has been laid (House of Commons)
name: Statement informing the House that a proposal for a draft remedial order has been laid
description: Remedial orders are a type of statutory instrument. Under the Human Rights Act 1998, courts can declare Acts of Parliament incompatible with the European convention on human rights. The Government can use remedial orders to amend Acts to remove the incompatibility. A minister formally presents (or ‘lays’) a proposal for a draft remedial order. The Minister may also make a statement to both Houses informing them that a proposal has been laid.
actualisationnote: The statement will appear on the day of laying.  It may appear as a written statement or statement made on the floor of the House. It will be duplicated in the second House.
datenote: Date of written statement

description: Under House of Lords Standing Order 72, no motion for a resolution of the House to approve a remedial affirmative instrument shall be made unless the instrument has been considered by the Joint Committee on Human Rights.  Standing Order 72 can be dispensed with to allow motions to approve affirmative instruments. This usually only applies to emergency legislation.
actualisationnote: Suspension of this SO removes the bar on moving an approval motion before reporting. In other words, the approval motion can be be moved despite the JCHR not having yet reported.

label: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law tabled (House of Lords)
name: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law tabled
description: A statutory instrument laid under the draft affirmative procedure cannot be made into law (signed by the minister) if the House of Lords approves a motion to stop the instrument becoming law.
actualisationnote: Will usually appear as the House 'declines to approve the instrument' and will appear in the LBP after laying of an instrument or as an amendment to the approval motion upon scheduling on a debate.
linknote: LBP - ideally the individual link but if missed then the Lords pdf for that day.
datenote: Date tabled

label: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law withdrawn (House of Lords)
name: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law withdrawn
description: A statutory instrument laid under the draft affirmative procedure cannot be made into law (signed by the minister) if the House of Lords approves a motion to stop the instrument becoming law. Such a motion can be withdrawn at any time after it has been tabled.
actualisationnote: A motion can be withdrawn at any point after tabling but usually happens immediately after debate. If withdrawn before debate takes place then the motion will no longer appear in LBP or scheduled.
linknote: LBP if withdrawn after debate. No link if withdrawn beforehand.
datenote: Date withdrawn

label: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law not moved (House of Lords)
name: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law not moved
description: A statutory instrument laid under the draft affirmative procedure cannot be made into law (signed by the minister) if the House of Lords approves a motion to stop the instrument becoming law.
actualisationnote: Usually occurs at the end of a debate considering several motions in one, the first will be decided while the others, where necessary, not moved
datenote: Date the motion was not moved

label: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law not called (House of Lords)
name: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law not called
description: A statutory instrument laid under the draft affirmative procedure cannot be made into law (signed by the minister) if the House of Lords approves a motion to stop the instrument becoming law.  A motion not having been called is when the motion is on the Order Paper for that day but did not occur. It may or may not be called on a future date.
actualisationnote: A motion not being called will usually mean it is on the Order Paper to be debated or moved (either alone or en bloc with similar motions) but for whatever reason the motion has not been called at all. We notice it by its omission, a comparison of what is due to happen on the Order Paper and what did happen in the Lords Minutes of Proceedings.
datenote: Date the motion was due to be called in the House of Lords chamber

label: Question on motion to stop the instrument becoming law put (House of Lords)
name: Question on motion to stop the instrument becoming law put
description: A statutory instrument laid under the draft affirmative procedure cannot be made into law (signed by the minister) if the House of Lords approves a motion to stop the instrument becoming law.
actualisationnote: In the draft affirmative procedure, a decision on a motion to amend the approval motion. Usually follows a debate in the Chamber or will be a separate motion if debated in GC
datenote: Date of decision

label: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law rejected (House of Lords)
name: Motion to stop the instrument becoming law rejected
description: A statutory instrument laid under the draft affirmative procedure cannot be made into law (signed by the minister) if the House of Lords approves a motion to stop the instrument becoming law.

label: Motion to stop the instrument being law tabled (House of Lords)
name: Motion to stop the instrument being law tabled
description: A statutory instrument laid under the made affirmative procedure becomes law on the day the Minister signs it and automatically remains law unless a motion to reject it is agreed by the House of Lords within the approval period.
actualisationnote: Motion text will refer to "be not made" or "Declines to approve" or "annuls".
linknote: LBP individual link or if not quick enough Lords PDF
datenote: Date tabled. This is not the same as date of notice (aka appearance in LBP).  Date tabled will be date of notice -1, Tuesday to Thursday. When date of notice falls on Monday, it will be the previous Friday irrespective of whether the previous Friday was non-sitting day. Where Date of Notice is first day back following a recess: date tabled tbc

label: Motion to stop the instrument being law withdrawn (House of Lords)
name: Motion to stop the instrument being law withdrawn
description: A statutory instrument laid under the made affirmative procedure becomes law on the day the Minister signs it and automatically remains law unless a motion to reject it is agreed by the House of Lords within the approval period. A motion can be withdrawn at any point between it being tabled and moved.
actualisationnote: Will occur either at the end of debate or at any point before debate (need to check LBP regularly to keep track of the motion itself)

It may or may not be called on a future date.

name: House resolved against committee procedure recommendation
description: The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the minister has recommended the appropriate level of scrutiny and can require a higher level. It has 30 days to do this. The Government, or member, can table a motion to disagree with the recommendation. If the House resolves against the committee recommendation then the Minister's recommendation stands.
actualisationnote: Following a debate there may be a question put to the House.
datenote: Date of decision

name: Government response to the procedure recommendation by the Regulatory Reform Committee (RRC)
description: The Government are expected to reply to the recommendations in a select committee report within two months of publication.
actualisationnote: Government responses will either be published as a special report by the select committee itself or laid as a command paper.
linknote: If a command paper link to the page on gov.uk. If a special report by the select committee link to the html version.
datenote: If a command paper use date of laying. If a special report by the select committee use date of the V&P.

actualisationnote: The Regulatory Reform Committee meets on an adhoc basis so we should be keeping an eye on the Vote for any reports. Note that since 2021, the Committee has been dissolved and its remit transferred to the Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee
datenote: Date the committee met, not the date the report was published

label: House of Commons agreed to the negative procedure (House of Commons)
name: House of Commons agreed to the negative procedure
description: The way legislative reform orders are scrutinised is not fixed. When ministers lay a draft legislative reform order, they propose what level of scrutiny it should face. For the House of Commons to agree to the affirmative procedure a committee can either agree with the Minister's recommendation or the House can resolve against a Committee's decision to upgrade to an affirmative procedure.
actualisationnote: Decision will be noted in a BEIS committee report which will appear on an adhoc basis.
datenote: Date the BEIS report on their decision on procedure

label: House of Commons agreed to the affirmative procedure (House of Commons)

For the House of Commons to agree to the affirmative procedure a committee can either agree with the Minister's recommendation, or the committee may decide the recommended negative procedure should be upgraded to the affirmative or the House can resolve against a Committee's decision to upgrade to a super-affirmative procedure.
actualisationnote: Decision will be noted in a BEIS committee report which will appear on an adhoc basis.

actualisationnote: It is possible that the DPRRC may publish further information (correspondence or oral evidence) after they have made a decision on an instrument, if this happens please check with Jane White before actualising this step.
datenote: Date of meeting, not publishing.

label: Oral evidence session by the International Trade Committee (ITC) (House of Commons)
name: Oral evidence session by the International Trade Committee (ITC)
description: The International Trade Committee has the power to invite Ministers and other experts to oral evidence sessions before or after they have considered a treaty.
actualisationnote: The ITC have the power to invite Ministers to oral evidence sessions before or after they have considered a treaty.  The evidence session should appear in the Vote under the inquiry name or as an adhoc piece.
linknote: HTML version should be used unless a pdf is only available
datenote: Date of the session, not the date the transcript was published.

label: Preliminary report by the International Trade Committee (ITC) (House of Commons)
name: Preliminary report by the International Trade Committee (ITC)
description: The International Trade Committee (ITC) can make a number of recommendations to Parliament about a treaty subject to the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 including, but not limited to, that the treaty should not be ratified, that a debate be scheduled or that the objection period should be extended. In some cases the International Trade Committee may report preliminary findings before the inquiry has completed or while they await further information.
actualisationnote: Any Commons select committee can consider a treaty and make recommendations
datenote: Date select committee met, not date of report publishing

description: Consideration completed by the Joint Committee on Human Rights (JCHR) within their terms of reference. The Committee can make several recommendations from whether the remedial order is appropriate, whether it should be changed or proceed in the same terms. The Committee also makes recommendations on whether the House(s) should approve the order.
actualisationnote: The JCHR meets on an adhoc basis so we should be keeping an eye on the Vote and Lords Minutes for any reports.
datenote: Date the committee met, not the date the report was published

label: Urgent question on the treaty (House of Commons)
name: Urgent question on the treaty
description: If an urgent or important matter arises which an MP believes requires an immediate answer from a government minister, they may apply to ask an urgent question.
actualisationnote: This step is not part of the specific CRaG procedure for treaties, it was added to the procedure map to allow it to be shown on the treaty timeline for user information. Urgent questions can happen to a treaty before it is laid for CRaG purposes.  It is not usual practice to add such a step if it is not part of the procedure. What is part of a specific procedure and what is procedurally possible within the Chamber are not always mutually exclusive.
datenote: Date the question took place

label: Instrument created under the United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020
name: Instrument created under the United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020
description: Affirmative instruments laid under the United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020 are subject to the Minister seeking legislative consent from the devolved authorities.
actualisationnote: Only actualised for affirmative instruments laid under the United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020. It replaces the normal 'Instrument created' step. These SIs are identified by the existence of a written statement or in the explanatory memorandum.
datenote: Made date or laid date if draft

label: Written statement explaining why the Government decided to make the regulations without the consent of the devolved authorities (House of Commons)
name: Written statement explaining why the Government decided to make the regulations without the consent of the devolved authorities
description: Affirmative instruments laid under section 10 of the United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020 are subject to the Minister seeking legislative consent from the devolved authorities. If the Minister does not seek consent from one or more of the devolved authorities they must make a statement to Parliament explaining why consent was not sought.
actualisationnote: The written statement will appear on the day of laying. It will appear in the House of Commons and duplicated in the House of Lords.
datenote: Date of written statement

If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision.
actualisationnote: This is likely to be a Government motion so will appear on the Order paper.

datenote: Date it first appeared - if unsure contact Lords Table Office.

If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision. The motion can be withdrawn at any point between tabling and the question being put.
actualisationnote: This may be in the Lords Motions Relating to Delegated Legislation section of Lords Business by way of omission when it previously existed. If a motion is withdrawn after debate then the Lords Minute will be the source.

description: The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the draft legislative reform order should be approved.  If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision. All motions lapse at the end of the session if they are still on the table.
actualisationnote: All motions lapse at the end of session.

name: Motion to disagree with the decision made by the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee (DPRRC) not moved
description: The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the draft legislative reform order should be approved.  If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision. Members or Government can choose not to move a motion before or after debate.
actualisationnote: Usually occurs at the end of a debate considering several motions in one, the first will be decided while the others, where necessary, not moved
datenote: Date the motion was not moved

label: Motion to disagree with the decision made by the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee (DPRRC) not called (House of Lords)
name: Motion to disagree with the decision made by the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee (DPRRC) not called
description: The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the draft legislative reform order should be approved.  If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision. A motion not having been called is when the motion is on the Order Paper for that day but did not occur. It may or may not be called on a future date.
actualisationnote: A motion not being called will usually mean it is on the Order Paper to be debated or moved (either alone or en bloc with similar motions) but for whatever reason the motion has not been called at all. We notice it by its omission, a comparison of what is due to happen on the Order Paper and what did happen on the Lords Minutes.
datenote: Date the motion was due to be called in the House of Lords chamber

If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision. Such a motion should be debated before the question is put.

label: No concerns raised by the Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee (House of Commons)
name: No concerns raised by the Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee
description: The Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the draft legislative reform order should be approved. If the committee is satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order can proceed.
actualisationnote: The BEIS Committee meets on an adhoc basis so we should be keeping an eye on the Vote for any reports.
datenote: Date the committee met, not the date the report was published

label: Concerns raised by the Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee (House of Commons)
name: Concerns raised by the Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee
description: The Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the draft legislative reform order should be approved. The committee may be satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act but raised concerns in the line with their terms of reference.
actualisationnote: The BEIS Committee meets on an adhoc basis so we should be keeping an eye on the Vote for any reports.
datenote: Date the committee met, not the date the report was published

label: Ministerial statement announcing a treaty has been signed (House of Lords)
name: Ministerial statement announcing a treaty has been signed
description: A Minister can make a statement to the House in the form of an oral statement or written statement to inform the House that a treaty has been signed with a trading partner.
actualisationnote: For such statements they can happen in the form of a written statement or a ministerial statement made in the Chamber. Statements are normally duplicated in the other House.
linknote: Written statement website link or Hansard if a spoken statement.
datenote: Date of statement

label: Motion to disagree with the decision made by the Commons committee withdrawn (House of Commons)
name: Motion to disagree with the decision made by the Commons committee withdrawn
description: The Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the draft legislative reform order should be approved. If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision. The motion can be withdrawn at any point between tabling and the question being put.
actualisationnote: This may be in the Commons Future Business by way of omission when it previously existed. If a motion is withdrawn after debate then the Votes will be the source.
linknote: No link? Or link to Vote if the motion was withdrawn after debate.
datenote: Date withdrawn

label: Motion to disagree with the decision made by the Commons committee not called (House of Commons)
name: Motion to disagree with the decision made by the Commons committee not called
description: The Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the draft legislative reform order should be approved. If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision. A motion not having been called is when the motion is on the Order Paper for that day but did not occur. It may or may not be called on a future date.
actualisationnote: A motion not being called will usually mean it is on the Order Paper to be debated or moved (either alone or en bloc with similar motions) but for whatever reason the motion has not been called at all. We notice it by its omission, a comparison of what is due to happen on the Order Paper and what did happen on the Votes & Proceedings.
datenote: Date the motion was due to be called in the House of Commons chamber

label: Question put on motion to disagree with the decision made by the Commons committee (House of Commons)
name: Question put on motion to disagree with the decision made by the Commons committee
description: The Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the draft legislative reform order should be approved. If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision.
actualisationnote: Following a debate there may be a question put to the House.
datenote: Date of question

label: House resolved against committee decision (House of Commons)
name: House resolved against committee decision
description: The Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the draft legislative reform order should be approved. If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision. If the House resolves against the committee decision then proceedings can continue on the order.
actualisationnote: Following a debate there may be a question put to the House.
datenote: Date of decision

label: House resolved in favour of committee decision (House of Commons)
name: House resolved in favour of committee decision
description: The Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the draft legislative reform order should be approved. If the committee is not satisfied that the Order meets the tests set out in the 2006 Act then the order cannot proceed. The Government or a member can table a motion to disagree with the committee's decision. If the House resolves in favour of the committee decision then proceedings cannot continue and therefore the instrument cannot be made.
actualisationnote: Following a debate there may be a question put to the House.
datenote: Date of decision

label: Instrument has been determined to follow the affirmative procedure (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Instrument has been determined to follow the affirmative procedure
description: A legislative reform order has been determined to follow the affirmative procedure if the minister has recommended such a procedure and a neither House has agreed to upgrade the order to follow the super-affirmative procedure. Or the minister has recommended the negative procedure and a committee of either House has agreed to upgrade the order to follow the affirmative procedure.
actualisationnote: Once one House agrees to the affirmative procedure and the other does not upgrade to the super-affirmative procedure we can actualise the final decision to make it clear which procedure the LRO will proceed with.
datenote: Date the second House makes a decision on the procedure.

label: Instrument has been determined to follow the super-affirmative procedure (House of Commons, House of Lords)
name: Instrument has been determined to follow the super-affirmative procedure
description: A legislative reform order has been determined to follow the super-affirmative procedure if the minister has recommended such a procedure. Or the minister has recommended the affirmative procedure and a committee of either House has agreed to upgrade the order to follow the super-affirmative procedure.
actualisationnote: Once one House agrees to the super-affirmative  can actualise the final decision to make it clear which procedure the LRO will proceed with.
datenote: Date the first House agrees to the super-affirmative procedure

label: Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee (DPRRC) agreed the instrument should follow the super-affirmative procedure (House of Lords)
name: Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee (DPRRC) agreed the instrument should follow the super-affirmative procedure
description: The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee  examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the minister has recommended the appropriate level of scrutiny and can require a higher level. It has 30 days to do this.
actualisationnote: Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee meet on an adhoc basis so indexers should be keeping an eye on Lords business and these reports to see whether LROs have been considered.
linknote: HTML version of DPRRC report - use an anchor link to exact location in report if possible
datenote: Date DPRRC met, not the day report published.

label: Motion to disagree with the procedure recommendation made by the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee (DPRRC) tabled (House of Lords)
name: Motion to disagree with the procedure recommendation made by the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee (DPRRC) tabled
description: The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee examines all legislative reform orders. It considers whether the minister has recommended the appropriate level of scrutiny and can require a higher level. It has 30 days to do this. The Government, or member, can table a motion to disagree with the recommendation.
actualisationnote: Such a motion can be tabled at any point after the committee has reported its decision.
linknote: LBP - Motions Relating to Delegated Legislation or Future Business
datenote: Date tabled. This is not the same as date of  notice (aka appearance in LBP).  Date tabled will be date of notice -1, Tuesday to Thursday. When date of notice falls on Monday, it will be the previous Friday irrespective of whether the previous Friday was non-sitting day. Where Date of Notice is first day back following a recess: date tabled tbc

label: Motion tabled that Standing Order 73 be dispensed with to enable motion to approve to be moved (House of Lords)

description: Under House of Lords Standing Order 72, no motion for a resolution of the House to approve an affirmative reform order shall be made unless the instrument has been considered by the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee. Standing Order 72 can be dispensed with to allow motions to approve affirmative instruments. This usually only applies to emergency legislation.
actualisationnote: Suspension of this SO removes the bar on tabling an approval motion before reporting. In other words, the approval motion can be be moved despite the DPRRC not having yet reported.
